title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Fork clone repository: Install dependencies: Build test:
code: git clone https://github.com/zdebruine/RcppML.git cd RcppML install.packages(c("Rcpp", "RcppEigen", "Matrix", "testthat", "devtools", "roxygen2", "knitr", "rmarkdown")) devtools::document() devtools::test() devtools::check()

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Edit files R/ roxygen2 comments (#' @param, #' @export, etc.) Regenerate documentation: Fix Rcpp info bug: Run tests:
code: devtools::document() bash tools/fix_rcpp_info_bug.sh devtools::test()

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: C++ algorithm code lives inst/include/ header-library (subdivided RcppML/, FactorNet/, streampress/, sparsepress/). src/ directory contains Rcpp bridge files (RcppFunctions*.cpp) provide // [[Rcpp::export]] wrappers, plus sparsepress_bridge.cpp StreamPress /O. changing // [[Rcpp::export]] annotations, run: :
code: devtools::document()  # Also runs Rcpp::compileAttributes() bash tools/fix_rcpp_info_bug.sh

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Follow standard R style (snake_case functions, PascalCase S4 classes) exported functions must complete roxygen documentation including @return @examples Use stop() user-facing errors clear messages

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Namespace: RcppML (PascalCase) new code Templates: use Scalar floating-point type parameter Headers must Rcpp-free — use RCPPML_LOG_INFO() instead Rcpp::Rcout Sparse matrices: expect Eigen::SparseMatrix<Scalar> CSC format Internal W stored transposed (k × m) cache efficiency

title: Contributing to RcppML
code: devtools::test()                                    # All tests testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test_nmf.R")    # Single file

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Place test files tests/testthat/ named test_*.R Use skip_if_not_installed() optional dependencies Use skip_if(Sys.getenv("RCPPML_SKIP_GPU") == "true") GPU tests Ground truth recovery tests use multi-restart robustness

title: Contributing to RcppML

title: Contributing to RcppML
text: Create feature branch: git checkout -b feature/-feature Make changes tests Ensure devtools::check() passes errors Push open Pull Request main

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: NMF inherently soft clustering method. column H gives sample’s “membership” across k factors, taking argmax rows yields hard cluster assignment. However, NMF non-convex — different random initializations produce different solutions potentially different clusters. Consensus NMF addresses running many replicates, tracking samples consistently co-cluster, building robust consensus matrix. Beyond flat clustering, RcppML provides divisive hierarchical clustering (dclust) via recursive rank-2 NMF splits. core idea rank-2 NMF binary clustering: split partitions samples two groups based two factors dominates. dclust applies recursively, building full hierarchy — node tree bipartition() finds best binary split. Classification embeddings leverages low-dimensional H representation supervised tasks.

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: data — input matrix (features × samples) .spz path k — factorization rank (number clusters) reps — number NMF replicates (= stable; use ≤ 30 speed) method — "hard" assigns clusters dominant factor; "knn_jaccard" uses k-NN Jaccard overlap (robust ambiguity) Returns consensus_nmf object $consensus (n × n co-clustering matrix), $clusters (assignments), $cophenetic (stability metric), $models (fitted models). Use plot() consensus heatmap summary() cluster statistics.
code: consensus_nmf(data, k, reps = 50, method = c("hard", "knn_jaccard"),               knn = 10, seed = NULL, threads = 0, verbose = FALSE, ...)

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: bipartition() performs single rank-2 NMF split. dclust() recursively bipartitions clusters smaller min_samples modularity min_dist. Important: dclust() returns 0-indexed $samples. Add 1 R-style indexing.
code: bipartition(data, tol = 1e-5, nonneg = TRUE, ...) dclust(A, min_samples, min_dist = 0, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100,        nonneg = TRUE, seed = NULL, threads = 0, verbose = FALSE)

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: Hungarian algorithm optimal 1:1 factor correspondence given cost (dissimilarity) matrix. Returns 0-indexed $assignment total $cost. using cosine similarity, convert cost first: bipartiteMatch(1 - cosine_sim).
code: bipartiteMatch(x)

code: classify_embedding(embedding, labels, test_fraction = 0.2, k = 5L, seed = NULL) classify_logistic(embedding, labels, test_fraction = 0.2, seed = NULL)

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: consensus matrix CC defined element-wise: CijC_{ij} fraction replicates samples ii jj assigned cluster. Perfect clustering produces binary consensus (0 1); noisy unstable clustering yields intermediate values. cophenetic correlation measures well hierarchical clustering consensus matrix preserves pairwise distances. Higher values indicate stable clustering. Divisive clustering recursively applies rank-2 NMF. split finds best binary partition; process stops clusters small homogeneous (low modularity). Classification H: Columns H k-dimensional embeddings samples. classifier (k-NN, logistic regression) applied embeddings can separate classes, assuming captured NMF factors.

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: Golub leukemia dataset contains 38 bone marrow samples — 27 11 AML — measured across 5,000 genes. Can unsupervised NMF discover known subtypes?  consensus heatmap reveals two tightly co-clustered groups. Let’s check well correspond known /AML labels. Consensus cluster assignments vs. true cancer type (/AML) Reading confusion matrix: row consensus cluster, column true cancer type. well-separated clustering aligns row predominantly one column. , Cluster 1 captures nearly samples Cluster 2 captures AML samples. overall purity (fraction samples assigned majority class) 0.895. Consensus NMF summary statistics Consensus NMF k = 2 cleanly separates two leukemia subtypes. cophenetic correlation confirms high clustering stability across replicates.
code: data(golub) labels <- attr(golub, "cancer_type")  # Consensus NMF: samples (rows) x genes (columns) cons <- consensus_nmf(golub, k = 2, reps = 20, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE) plot(cons, show_clusters = TRUE) +   ggtitle("Consensus matrix: Golub leukemia (k = 2, 20 replicates)") # Confusion matrix: consensus clusters vs. true labels conf <- table(Cluster = paste0("Cluster ", cons$clusters), Cancer = labels) knitr::kable(conf, caption = "Consensus cluster assignments vs. true cancer type (ALL/AML)") summary_df <- data.frame(   Metric = c("Cophenetic correlation", "Cluster 1 size", "Cluster 2 size"),   Value = c(round(cons$cophenetic, 4),             sum(cons$clusters == 1),             sum(cons$clusters == 2)) ) knitr::kable(summary_df, caption = "Consensus NMF summary statistics")

title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: happens push beyond known two classes? k = 5, consensus NMF discovers finer-grained substructure within AML.  k = 5 consensus clusters vs. cancer type: substructure within AML k = 5, consensus matrix reveals AML contain subpopulations — splits 2–3 subgroups AML 2. cophenetic correlation 0.999 confirms stable cluster boundaries even finer resolution. substructure biologically meaningful: includes B-cell T-cell subtypes, AML harbors distinct genetic programs.
code: cons5 <- consensus_nmf(golub, k = 5, reps = 20, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE) plot(cons5, show_clusters = TRUE) +   ggtitle("Consensus matrix: Golub leukemia (k = 5, 20 replicates)") conf5 <- table(Cluster = paste0("C", cons5$clusters), Cancer = labels) knitr::kable(conf5, caption = "k = 5 consensus clusters vs. cancer type: substructure within ALL and AML")

what: Example 2: Hierarchical Clustering with dclust (AML Methylation)
title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: AML dataset contains 824 differentially methylated regions (DMRs) beta-value measurements across 135 samples known subtype annotations (AML matched controls MEP, GMP, L-MPP progenitor stages). Divisive clustering discovers hierarchy subtypes recursively applying rank-2 NMF splits. Divisive clustering AML methylation data Divisive clustering discovers hierarchy subtypes, early splits separating biologically distinct cell populations. cluster’s composition reflects known AML subtype structure.
code: data(aml) meta <- attr(aml, "metadata_h")  clusters <- dclust(aml, min_samples = 10, min_dist = 0.01, seed = 42)  # Build cluster composition table cluster_info <- do.call(rbind, lapply(clusters, function(cl) {   sample_idx <- cl$samples + 1L  # 0-indexed to 1-indexed   categories <- meta$category[sample_idx]   top_cats <- sort(table(categories), decreasing = TRUE)   data.frame(     Cluster = cl$id,     Size = cl$size,     `Top Category` = names(top_cats)[1],     `Top Count` = as.integer(top_cats[1]),     `Composition` = paste(paste0(names(top_cats)[1:min(3, length(top_cats))], ":",                                   as.integer(top_cats[1:min(3, length(top_cats))])),                           collapse = ", "),     check.names = FALSE   ) }))  knitr::kable(head(cluster_info, 8), caption = "Divisive clustering of AML methylation data")

previous_headings: Example 2: Hierarchical Clustering with dclust (AML Methylation)
title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: Cluster labels encode splitting path binary strings: character records whether node went left (“0”) right (“1”). root split (depth 0) produces children “0” “1”. Splits depth 1 subdivide : “0” → “00” (left) “01” (right), . string length minus one gives split depth cluster created.  top panel shows true splitting tree branch points corresponding individual bipartition() calls. bottom panel shows composition leaf cluster stacked bar, aligned tree . root split (depth 0) separates biologically distinct cell populations, deeper splits resolve finer substructure within AML subtypes.
code: plot(clusters, labels = meta$category,      main = "Divisive Hierarchy with Subtype Composition")

what: Example 3: Classification from NMF Embeddings (AML Subtypes)
title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: AML methylation data provides realistic multi-class classification challenge: 6 subtypes imbalanced class sizes. NMF embedding compresses 824 DMRs k factors; classify subtypes low-dimensional representation. Classification NMF embeddings (k = 8, AML subtypes) k-NN confusion matrix (AML subtypes) Unlike two-class Golub problem (NMF achieves near-perfect separation classifier), AML’s 6 subtypes imbalanced class sizes harder classify 8 factors. confusion matrix shows subtypes well-resolved confused.
code: # Fit NMF to AML data (features x samples) model <- nmf(aml, k = 8, seed = 42, maxit = 100) embedding <- t(model@h)  # 135 samples x 8 factors int_labels <- as.integer(factor(meta$category)) - 1L  # 0-indexed  # 20% held-out test set set.seed(42) n <- nrow(embedding) test_idx <- sort(sample(n, floor(n * 0.2)))  knn_eval <- classify_embedding(embedding, int_labels, test_idx = test_idx, k = 5, seed = 42) log_eval <- classify_logistic(embedding, int_labels, test_idx = test_idx, seed = 42)  comp_table <- data.frame(   Method = c("k-NN (k=5)", "Logistic regression"),   Accuracy = c(knn_eval$accuracy, log_eval$accuracy),   `Macro F1` = c(knn_eval$macro_f1, log_eval$macro_f1),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(comp_table, digits = 3,              caption = "Classification from NMF embeddings (k = 8, AML subtypes)") # k-NN confusion matrix conf_knn <- knn_eval$confusion cat_levels <- levels(factor(meta$category)) if (nrow(conf_knn) == length(cat_levels)) {   rownames(conf_knn) <- cat_levels   colnames(conf_knn) <- cat_levels } knitr::kable(conf_knn, caption = "k-NN confusion matrix (AML subtypes)")

previous_headings: Example 3: Classification from NMF Embeddings (AML Subtypes)
title: Clustering, Consensus, and Classification
text: UMAP projection reveals well separated subtypes 8-dimensional NMF embedding space. Tight, isolated groups indicate subtypes classify well; overlapping groups indicate subtypes share methylation structure.
code: library(uwot) set.seed(42) umap_coords <- umap(embedding, n_neighbors = 15, min_dist = 0.3, n_components = 2)  umap_df <- data.frame(   UMAP1 = umap_coords[, 1],   UMAP2 = umap_coords[, 2],   Subtype = meta$category,   Set = ifelse(seq_len(n) %in% test_idx, "Test", "Train") )  ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = Subtype, shape = Set)) +   geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   scale_shape_manual(values = c(Train = 16, Test = 4)) +   labs(title = "UMAP of NMF Embeddings (AML Subtypes)",        subtitle = "8-factor NMF embedding colored by subtype, shaped by train/test split") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: many factors data really ? Training loss always decreases kk increases — adding factors can improve fit training data, even extra factors fitting noise. makes train loss useless selecting optimal rank. RcppML solves speckled holdout cross-validation: randomly mask fraction individual matrix entries, fit NMF model remaining entries, evaluate reconstruction error held-entries. Unlike row column holdout, speckled masking preserves full structure every sample every feature. optimal rank kk test loss curve forms “elbow” — decreasing sharply true rank flattening increasing thereafter. Cross-validation also one computationally intensive operations NMF, since requires fitting multiple ranks across multiple replicates. GPU acceleration can provide substantial speedups (10–50×) large-scale CV sweeps — see GPU Acceleration vignette.

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Cross-validation triggered passing vector k: Return value: data.frame class nmfCrossValidate columns: Use plot(cv_result) built-test-loss-vs-rank curve.
code: nmf(data, k = c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10),     test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 1:3,     mask = NULL, patience = 5, ...)

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: mask parameter controls fitting cross-validation simultaneously — separate CV-specific mask setting. accepts five forms: mask = NULL (default): entries (including zeros) can held . Tests full-matrix reconstruction. Appropriate dense data applications zero real measurement. mask = "zeros": nonzero entries can held . Zeros treated “unobserved,” “zero-valued.” Essential recommendation data (unrated items) domain zero means “measured.” mask = "NA": NA entries data treated missing. data contains NAs mask specified, set automatically warning. mask = <matrix>: custom dgCMatrix matrix dimensions data. Nonzero entries mask mark observed positions; zero entries mark unobserved positions. mask = list("zeros", <matrix>): Combines zero-masking custom mask — zeros treated unobserved custom mask applied simultaneously. Caution: compare test loss values across mask modes axis. mask = "zeros", test set includes non-zero entries (e.g., actual ratings), producing loss values natural scale. mask = NULL, test set dominated zero entries, yielding much smaller absolute loss values. two scales fundamentally different direct comparison misleading.

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Different cv_seed values produce different holdout masks different NMF initializations. Averaging test loss across replicates smooths variability sources. Use 2–3 replicates stable results.

what: Example 1: Recovering True Rank from Synthetic Data
title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: generate matrix exactly 5 true factors noise, ask cross-validation find optimal rank. Mean test loss per rank (3 replicates). Optimal k = 5. Cross-validation identifies optimal rank — test loss drops sharply k=2 k=5 (true rank), increases beyond k=5 model begins fitting noise rather structure.  clear elbow k=5 confirms speckled holdout CV recovers planted rank. individual replicate points cluster tightly, showing 3 replicates suffice stable estimates matrix size.
code: sim <- simulateNMF(200, 80, k = 5, noise = 3.0, seed = 42) cv <- nmf(sim$A, k = 2:15, test_fraction = 0.05, cv_seed = 1:3,           tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200) agg <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv, FUN = mean) agg$se <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv, FUN = function(x) sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))$test_mse optimal_k <- agg$k[which.min(agg$test_mse)]  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     k = agg$k,     `Mean Test Loss` = format(agg$test_mse, digits = 3, scientific = TRUE),     `SE` = format(agg$se, digits = 3, scientific = TRUE),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = paste0("Mean test loss per rank (3 replicates). Optimal k = ", optimal_k, ".") ) plot(cv) +   labs(title = "Cross-Validation: Test Loss vs. Rank (Synthetic Data, True k = 5)") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Cross-validation works overfitting worsens higher ranks: factors mean capacity memorize training data. can visualize tracking test loss every iteration across multiple ranks, using on_iteration callback capture per-epoch dynamics. epoch plot displays rank x-axis test loss y-axis, lines colored iteration. Early iterations (bright) show high test loss ranks; training progresses (dark), optimal rank emerges one steepest improvement.  line traces single training epoch across ranks. optimal rank, test loss drops furthest training progresses. excessively high ranks, diminishing improvement (rising test loss later epochs) signals overfitting — extra capacity fitting noise rather structure. y-axis clipped 10× minimum test loss focus informative range.
code: data(hawaiibirds) ranks_to_show <- 2:20  # Capture per-iteration test loss at each rank track_test <- function() {   losses <- numeric(0)   list(     callback = function(iter, train_loss, test_loss) { losses[iter] <<- test_loss },     get = function() losses   ) }  iter_logs <- do.call(rbind, lapply(ranks_to_show, function(k_val) {   tracker <- track_test()   nmf(hawaiibirds, k = k_val, test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 1, seed = 42,       tol = 0, maxit = 30, patience = 30, on_iteration = tracker$callback)   losses <- tracker$get()   data.frame(k = k_val, iter = seq_along(losses), test_loss = losses) })) # Bound y-axis: from min test loss to 10x that min_loss <- min(iter_logs$test_loss) y_lo <- min_loss * 0.95 y_hi <- min_loss * 10  ggplot(iter_logs, aes(x = k, y = test_loss, color = iter, group = iter)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.7, linewidth = 0.6) +   geom_point(size = 1.5) +   scale_color_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1) +   scale_y_continuous(trans = "log10", limits = c(y_lo, y_hi)) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2, 20, by = 2)) +   labs(     title = "Overfitting Dynamics: Test Loss by Rank and Iteration",     subtitle = "Hawaiian birds data (k = 2:20) — later epochs (dark) reveal the optimal rank",     x = "Rank (k)", y = "Test Loss (log scale)", color = "Iteration"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: movielens dataset (3,867 movies × 610 users) contains star ratings 1–5. entries zero — meaning user rated movie, gave zero stars. mask = "zeros" setting essential : restricts holdout set actual ratings treats zeros unobserved.  MovieLens CV results (mask = ‘zeros’). Optimal k = 35. mask = "zeros", cross-validation evaluates prediction actual ratings — exactly task recommendation system performs.
code: data(movielens)  cat("MovieLens dimensions:", nrow(movielens), "×", ncol(movielens), "\n") #> MovieLens dimensions: 3867 × 610 cat("Sparsity:", round(1 - Matrix::nnzero(movielens) / prod(dim(movielens)), 4) * 100, "%\n") #> Sparsity: 96.81 %  ranks <- seq(5, 50, by = 5) cv_movielens <- nmf(movielens, k = ranks, test_fraction = 0.1,                     mask = "zeros", cv_seed = 1:3, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 100) agg_ml <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_movielens, FUN = mean)  plot(cv_movielens) +   labs(     title = "Cross-Validation on MovieLens (mask = 'zeros')",     subtitle = "Test loss evaluated only on actual ratings — zeros are unobserved"   ) +   theme_minimal() agg_ml$se <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_movielens, FUN = function(x) sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))$test_mse opt_ml <- agg_ml$k[which.min(agg_ml$test_mse)]  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     k = agg_ml$k,     `Mean Test Loss` = round(agg_ml$test_mse, 4),     `SE` = round(agg_ml$se, 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = paste0("MovieLens CV results (mask = 'zeros'). Optimal k = ", opt_ml, ".") )

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: hawaiibirds dataset records 183 bird species across 1,183 grid cells. ecological surveys, zeros typically mean “species detected” rather “measured abundance zero.” use default mask = NULL want evaluate model’s ability reconstruct full abundance matrix, including correctly predicting many species absent many sites.
code: data(hawaiibirds)  ranks <- c(3, 5, 8, 12, 16) cv_birds <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = ranks, test_fraction = 0.1,                 cv_seed = 1:3, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 50) plot(cv_birds) +   labs(title = "Cross-Validation on Hawaiian Birds (default mask)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: compare different holdout fractions show affect rank selection stability. true planted rank 5. Effect test_fraction rank selection (synthetic data, true k = 5).  Rank selection robust across wide range holdout fractions. small fractions (1%) higher variance due small test set, large fractions (30%) leave less data training. Computational cost scales test fraction. Every masked entry requires per-iteration prediction loss evaluation current model, adding O(k)O(k) work per held-entry every epoch. Doubling test fraction roughly doubles overhead — large sparse matrices masked-entry bookkeeping can dominate total runtime. test fraction 1–5% usually sufficient stable rank selection keeping computational burden negligible relative NMF solve . Reserve larger fractions (10–20%) small matrices absolute number test entries otherwise reliable estimates.
code: fractions <- c(0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.3) fraction_results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(fractions, function(frac) {   cv_res <- nmf(sim$A, k = 2:15, test_fraction = frac, cv_seed = 1:3,                 tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100)   agg <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_res, FUN = mean)   se_vals <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_res, FUN = function(x) sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))$test_mse   data.frame(     test_fraction = frac,     optimal_k = agg$k[which.min(agg$test_mse)],     min_test_loss = min(agg$test_mse),     mean_se = mean(se_vals)   ) })) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     `Test Fraction` = fraction_results$test_fraction,     `Selected k` = fraction_results$optimal_k,     `Min Test Loss` = format(fraction_results$min_test_loss, digits = 3, scientific = TRUE),     `Mean SE` = format(fraction_results$mean_se, digits = 3, scientific = TRUE),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Effect of test_fraction on rank selection (synthetic data, true k = 5).",   row.names = FALSE ) ggplot(fraction_results, aes(x = test_fraction, y = min_test_loss)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "steelblue") +   geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +   geom_text(aes(label = paste0("k=", optimal_k)),             vjust = -1, size = 3.5) +   labs(     title = "Test Loss vs. Holdout Fraction (True k = 5)",     x = "Test Fraction", y = "Minimum Test Loss"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Distribution-aware CV: Use non-MSE loss functions count data. See Distributions vignette. Robust factorization: Combine CV-selected rank consensus_nmf() stable factors across multiple runs. See Clustering. GPU acceleration: Speed large CV sweeps 10–50× GPUs. See GPU Acceleration.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Standard NMF minimizes mean squared error, implicitly assuming Gaussian noise constant variance. assumption fails many real datasets: count data (gene expression, species surveys) variance proportional mean; heavy-tailed data extreme outliers dominate MSE objective; data excess zeros far beyond single distribution predicts (e.g., single-cell RNA-seq dropout). RcppML unifies distribution-specific NMF via Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS): NMF iteration, least squares subproblem re-weighted match chosen distribution’s variance function. result better fit, interpretable factors, statistically principled modeling.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Use loss parameter specify error distribution:
code: nmf(data, k, loss = "gp", ...)

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: dispersion parameter controls dispersion estimated:

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: data excess zeros beyond chosen distribution predicts:
code: nmf(data, k, loss = "gp", zi = "row", ...)

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: auto_nmf_distribution(data, k, distributions, criterion) — fit multiple distributions, compare via AIC/BIC score_test_distribution(data, model, powers) — score test variance power without refitting diagnose_zero_inflation(data, model, threshold) — test excess zeros diagnose_dispersion(data, model) — recommend dispersion granularity

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: distribution assumes specific relationship variance mean: V(μ)=μpV(\mu) = \mu^p. Gaussian (p=0) constant variance. Poisson-family (p=1) variance proportional mean. Gamma (p=2) variance proportional mean squared. correct assumption determines residuals weighted — high-mean entries get downweighted count data, matching natural heteroscedasticity.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: NMF iteration, IRLS computes weights wij=1/V(μ̂ij)w_{ij} = 1/V(\hat{\mu}_{ij}) solves weighted NNLS problem. iterative reweighting converges maximum likelihood estimate chosen distribution.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Dispersion controls much observed variance deviates base variance function. Negative Binomial, dispersion parameter θ\theta controls overdispersion: V(μ)=μ+μ2/θV(\mu) = \mu + \mu^2/\theta. Generalized Poisson, ϕ\phi controls extra-Poisson variation. three dispersion modes control granularity: per_row (default): feature (row) gets dispersion ϕi\phi_i. Appropriate features different noise levels — e.g., highly-expressed genes different variance structure lowly-expressed genes. per_col: sample (column) gets dispersion ϕj\phi_j. Appropriate samples different quality — e.g., cells different sequencing depth capture efficiency. global: single scalar dispersion ϕ\phi entire matrix. Appropriate homogeneous data features samples similar noise characteristics. Guidance: gene expression data, per_row typically best (genes vary enormously expression level noise). batch-variable data, per_col captures sample-level quality differences. Use diagnose_dispersion() choose empirically.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: ZI mixture model decomposes observation : P(X=0)=π+(1−π)⋅f(0|μ)P(X=0) = \pi + (1-\pi) \cdot f(0|\mu). EM algorithm alternates estimating zero-inflation probabilities π\pi updating NMF factors — capturing dropout structural zeros base distribution explain.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: modeling complex data, build complexity incrementally rather estimating everything simultaneously. Estimating distribution, dispersion, zero-inflation together creates identifiability issues — model may trade flexible distribution higher zero-inflation, producing unstable results. Select distribution: Use auto_nmf_distribution() score_test_distribution() find best variance function. Start dispersion = "none" zi = "none". Diagnose dispersion: Use diagnose_dispersion() determine whether per-row, per-col, global dispersion needed. Test zero-inflation: Use diagnose_zero_inflation() check excess zeros. Combine: Fit final model chosen distribution, dispersion mode, ZI setting. Tip: distribution parameter (available auto_nmf_distribution()) can set "auto" automatic distribution selection. evaluates multiple candidates selects best one specified criterion (default: BIC).

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Distribution comparison uses standard model selection criteria: NLL (Negative Log-Likelihood): negative log-likelihood data fitted model. Lower = better fit. AIC (Akaike Information Criterion): AIC=2⋅NLL+2k\text{AIC} = 2 \cdot \text{NLL} + 2k, kk number parameters. Penalizes model complexity lightly. BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion): BIC=2⋅NLL+klog(N)\text{BIC} = 2 \cdot \text{NLL} + k \log(N), NN number observations. Penalizes complexity strongly AIC, preferring simpler models. BIC generally preferred distribution selection. Note: NLL AIC/BIC values can negative — reflects scale log-likelihood, error.

what: Example 1: Distribution Auto-Selection on Count Data
title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: hawaiibirds dataset contains species frequency counts Hawaiian bird surveys — overdispersed count data Gaussian assumptions inappropriate. Distribution comparison hawaiibirds (BIC criterion). Best: mse.  BIC selects best-fitting distribution Hawaiian bird count data. Note BIC-preferred distribution may always count-based model: variance-mean relationship data closer constant (Gaussian), BIC prefer MSE complex alternatives. informative — tells whether additional complexity count-based loss warranted data. Higher BIC values count distributions indicate extra dispersion parameters justified improvement fit. Dispersion diagnostics hawaiibirds
code: data(hawaiibirds) result <- auto_nmf_distribution(hawaiibirds, k = 8,                                  distributions = c("mse", "gp", "nb"),                                  criterion = "bic", seed = 42,                                  maxit = 30) comp <- result$comparison comp_display <- data.frame(   Distribution = comp$distribution,   NLL = round(comp$nll, 1),   df = comp$df,   AIC = round(comp$aic, 1),   BIC = round(comp$bic, 1),   Selected = ifelse(comp$selected, "***", "") ) knitr::kable(   comp_display,   caption = paste0("Distribution comparison on hawaiibirds (BIC criterion). Best: ", result$loss, ".") ) ggplot(comp, aes(x = distribution, y = bic, fill = selected)) +   geom_col(width = 0.6) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +   labs(title = "BIC Comparison Across Distributions (Hawaiian Birds)",        x = "Distribution", y = "BIC (lower is better)") +   theme_minimal() # Diagnose dispersion granularity for the best model best_model <- result$models[[result$loss]] disp_diag <- diagnose_dispersion(hawaiibirds, best_model)  disp_df <- data.frame(   Property = c("Recommended mode", "Global φ", "Row CV", "Col CV"),   Value = c(disp_diag$mode, round(disp_diag$global_phi, 4),             round(disp_diag$row_cv, 4), round(disp_diag$col_cv, 4)) ) knitr::kable(disp_df, caption = "Dispersion diagnostics for hawaiibirds")

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: score test evaluates variance-power family without refitting — fast diagnostic determine distribution matches data’s variance structure. Score test results. Best power: p = 1 (gp). power smallest |T||T| best matches observed variance-mean relationship. score test evaluates variance power family V(μ)=μpV(\mu) = \mu^p: Power 0 (Gaussian): constant variance — rejected (large |T||T|), data heteroscedastic noise. Power 1 (Poisson/GP): variance proportional mean — appropriate count data. Power 2 (Gamma): variance proportional mean-squared — appropriate heavy-tailed continuous data. negative T-statistic given power suggests model -estimates variance relative data power — assumed variance function grows quickly mean. positive T-statistic suggests -estimation — data variance model predicts. best power one T-statistic closest zero — variance assumption matches reality. practice, small absolute T-statistics (< 0.1) across multiple powers suggest data’s variance-mean relationship well-behaved choice distribution matters less.
code: model_base <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 8, seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30) scores <- score_test_distribution(hawaiibirds, model_base) score_df <- scores$scores score_df$T_stat <- round(score_df$T_stat, 4) score_df$abs_T <- round(score_df$abs_T, 4) knitr::kable(   score_df,   caption = paste0("Score test results. Best power: p = ", scores$best_power,                     " (", scores$best_distribution, ").") ) if (!is.null(scores$nb_diagnostic)) {   nb_msg <- if (scores$nb_diagnostic$overdispersed) {     "Substantial overdispersion detected (T_NB > 0.1). NB or GP may be preferable to Poisson."   } else {     "No strong overdispersion beyond Poisson detected."   } }

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Single-cell RNA-seq data extreme sparsity “dropout” zeros beyond count distribution predicts. use pbmc3k, variance-selected subset (8,000 genes × 500 cells) full 10x Genomics PBMC 3k dataset. Dropout pervasive artifact scRNA-seq: library preparation, low-abundance mRNAs stochastically lost capture reverse-transcription steps. result genes moderate true expression frequently observed zero — cell lacks transcript, assay failed detect . produces “excess zeros” far beyond single count distribution (Poisson, NB, GP) can explain. Capture efficiency also varies across cells, contributing per-sample zero-inflation. sparse data excess zeros. example, hawaiibirds 97% sparse diagnose_zero_inflation() finds excess zero rate 1% — zeros well-explained count distribution . Zero-inflation modeling adds value diagnostic confirms structural excess zeros. dataset 93.8% zeros — far single count distribution can explain. Zero-inflation diagnostics pbmc3k (8,000 genes × 500 cells).
code: data(pbmc3k, package = "RcppML") tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, tmp) counts <- st_read(tmp)  sparsity_pct <- round(100 * (1 - Matrix::nnzero(counts) / prod(dim(counts))), 1) cat("Dimensions:", nrow(counts), "×", ncol(counts), "\n") #> Dimensions: 13714 × 2638 model_gp <- nmf(counts, k = 8, loss = "gp", seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30) zi_diag <- diagnose_zero_inflation(counts, model_gp) zi_summary <- data.frame(   Metric = c("Excess Zero Rate", "Zero-Inflation Detected", "Recommended ZI Mode"),   Value = c(     round(zi_diag$excess_zero_rate, 4),     as.character(zi_diag$has_zi),     zi_diag$zi_mode   ) ) knitr::kable(zi_summary, caption = "Zero-inflation diagnostics on pbmc3k (8,000 genes × 500 cells).") if (zi_diag$has_zi && zi_diag$zi_mode != "none") {   model_zi <- nmf(counts, k = 8, loss = "gp", zi = zi_diag$zi_mode,                   seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30)      loss_gp <- evaluate(model_gp, counts, loss = "mse")   loss_zi <- evaluate(model_zi, counts, loss = "mse")   improvement <- round(100 * (1 - loss_zi / loss_gp), 1)      loss_comp <- data.frame(     Model = c("GP", paste0("GP + ZI (", zi_diag$zi_mode, ")")),     `MSE` = round(c(loss_gp, loss_zi), 4),     check.names = FALSE   )   knitr::kable(loss_comp, caption = "Reconstruction error: GP vs. GP with zero-inflation.") }

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Rank selection distributions: Cross-validate loss = "gp" "nb". See Cross-Validation vignette. Factor interpretation: Combine distribution-aware NMF consensus clustering. See Clustering vignette. Core NMF mechanics: Gaussian/MSE NMF fundamentals, see NMF Fundamentals.

title: Factorization Graphs
text: nmf() decomposes one matrix two factors. covers lot ground — real problems often involve richer structure: Multi-modal data: Two matrices sharing samples share single latent space, factorized independently. Hierarchical decomposition: Coarse global patterns fine local patterns live different ranks — stacking NMF layers captures . Guided factorization: Prior knowledge (class labels, reference patterns) steer optimization, bolted afterward. Systematic tuning: Rank, regularization, loss function interact nonobvious ways — need principled way search together. RcppML’s factor_net() addresses treating factorization directed graph composable blocks. wire together inputs, layers, merge operations, fit() assembled network.

title: Factorization Graphs
text: Every graph compiled factor_net(inputs, output, config) executed fit(). result factor_net_result whose named elements give per-layer access W, H, d, loss, convergence info: Per-factor overrides regularization, constraints, target regularization set W() H():
code: inp <- factor_input(A, name = "data") out <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 8, name = "my_nmf") net <- factor_net(inp, out, factor_config(maxit = 100)) result <- fit(net)  result$my_nmf$W   # m x k result$my_nmf$H   # k x n result$my_nmf$d   # length-k scaling vector # Sparse W, with target regularization on H out <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 8,                  W = W(L1 = 0.1),                  H = H(target = T_mat, target_lambda = 0.3))

title: Factorization Graphs
text: simplest graph one input feeding one NMF layer — identical calling nmf(). Graph API matches plain nmf() exactly value graph API appears moment need something single nmf() call express.
code: data(aml)  # Plain nmf() set.seed(42) direct <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, maxit = 100)  # Same thing as a factor graph inp <- factor_input(aml, name = "methylation") out <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 6, name = "nmf1") net <- factor_net(inp, out, factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42)) graph_result <- fit(net)  # Compare W_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$W H_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$H d_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$d recon_graph <- W_gr %*% diag(d_gr) %*% H_gr mse_graph <- mean((as.matrix(aml) - recon_graph)^2)  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Method = c("nmf()", "factor_net"),     MSE = c(evaluate(direct, aml), mse_graph),     Iterations = c(direct@misc$iter, graph_result$nmf1$iterations),     check.names = FALSE   ),   digits = 6, caption = "Graph API matches plain nmf() exactly" )

title: Factorization Graphs
text: two matrices share nn samples, factor_shared() finds common latent space. vertically concatenates inputs fits one NMF: minW1,W2,H≥0∥[A1A2]−[W1W2]diag(d)H∥F2\min_{W_1, W_2, H \geq 0} \left\| \begin{bmatrix} A_1 \\ A_2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} W_1 \\ W_2 \end{bmatrix} \text{diag}(d)\,H \right\|_F^2 shared HH represents structure common views. WpW_p captures view-specific loadings.

title: Factorization Graphs
text: hawaiibirds dataset records 183 bird species across 1,183 survey grid cells 8 Hawaiian islands. cell also geographic coordinates. ’ll discover factors simultaneously explain species live places . shared HH assigns grid cell mixture 8 factors must explain bird communities geographic position:  geographic constraint encourages spatial coherence: factors activate neighboring islands share high activation, geographically distant islands get distinct factors.
code: data(hawaiibirds) meta_h <- attr(hawaiibirds, "metadata_h")  # View 1: species counts (183 x 1183, sparse) A_species <- hawaiibirds  # View 2: geographic coordinates (2 x 1183, dense) A_geo <- rbind(lat = scale(meta_h$lat)[, 1],                lng = scale(meta_h$lng)[, 1]) # Match magnitudes: geography as ~10% of total signal geo_weight <- 0.1 * sum(abs(A_species)) / sum(abs(A_geo)) A_geo_weighted <- as(A_geo * geo_weight, "dgCMatrix") inp_species <- factor_input(A_species, name = "species") inp_geo     <- factor_input(A_geo_weighted, name = "geography") shared_inp  <- factor_shared(inp_species, inp_geo) layer       <- nmf_layer(shared_inp, k = 8, name = "joint") config      <- factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42) net         <- factor_net(list(inp_species, inp_geo), layer, config) result      <- fit(net) H_shared <- result$joint$H island <- factor(meta_h$island)  # Mean factor activation per island activation <- sapply(levels(island), function(isl) {   rowMeans(H_shared[, island == isl, drop = FALSE]) })  # Normalize rows for display rmax <- apply(activation, 1, max) rmax[rmax == 0] <- 1 activation_norm <- activation / rmax  hm_df <- expand.grid(Factor = paste0("F", 1:8), Island = levels(island)) hm_df$Activation <- as.vector(activation_norm)  ggplot(hm_df, aes(x = Island, y = Factor, fill = Activation)) +   geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "steelblue") +   labs(title = "Shared Factor Activation by Island",        subtitle = "Each factor explains both species composition and geography",        fill = "Relative\nActivation") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Factorization Graphs
text: much geographic view change embedding? can measure agreement shared embedding species-NMF correlating HH factors.  Factors high correlation (r>0.9r > 0.9) look essentially without geography — capture species patterns visible counts alone. Factors lower correlation genuinely reshaped spatial constraint, surfacing structure species-NMF misses.
code: model_species_only <- nmf(A_species, k = 8, seed = 42, maxit = 100) H_species_only <- model_species_only@h  # Best absolute correlation between each shared factor and any species-only factor cross_cor <- cor(t(H_shared), t(H_species_only)) best_match <- apply(abs(cross_cor), 1, max)  cor_df <- data.frame(Factor = factor(1:8), Correlation = best_match) ggplot(cor_df, aes(x = Factor, y = Correlation)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0.9, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +   ylim(0, 1) +   labs(title = "Shared vs. Species-Only: Per-Factor Agreement",        subtitle = "Factors below the dashed line were reshaped by geographic information",        y = "Best |r| with species-only factor") +   theme_minimal()

title: Factorization Graphs
text: Stacking NMF layers extracts structure multiple granularities. first layer produces k1k_1-factor embedding; second factorizes embedding k2k_2 meta-factors: ≈W1diag(d1)H1,H1≈W2diag(d2)H2A \approx W_1\,\text{diag}(d_1)\,H_1, \qquad H_1 \approx W_2\,\text{diag}(d_2)\,H_2 H2H_2 captures coarse global structure; H1H_1 captures fine-grained detail.

title: Factorization Graphs
text: aml dataset 824 DNA methylation features across 135 samples 6 categories (3 AML subtypes + 3 control cell types). two-layer architecture first extracts 10 fine factors, compresses 3 meta-factors.  3 coarse meta-factors capture broad AML-vs-control distinction. 10 fine factors resolve subtypes — MEP vs. GMP vs. L-MPP within group. hierarchical view available single nmf() call either rank.
code: data(aml) meta_aml <- attr(aml, "metadata_h")  inp    <- factor_input(aml, name = "methylation") layer1 <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 10, name = "fine") layer2 <- nmf_layer(layer1, k = 3, name = "coarse") net    <- factor_net(inp, layer2, factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42)) deep   <- fit(net)  H_fine   <- deep$fine$H       # 10 x 135 (fine factors x samples) # For the coarse layer, the per-sample embedding is in W (135 x 3) # because the second layer factorizes H1: samples become "features" W_coarse <- deep$coarse$W     # 135 x 3 (samples x coarse factors) categories <- meta_aml$category cat_short <- gsub("Control ", "Ctrl-", gsub("AML ", "AML-", categories))  coarse_assign <- factor(paste0("C", apply(W_coarse, 1, which.max))) fine_assign   <- factor(paste0("F", apply(H_fine, 2, which.max)))  comp_df <- rbind(   data.frame(Factor = coarse_assign, Category = cat_short, Level = "Coarse (k=3)"),   data.frame(Factor = fine_assign,   Category = cat_short, Level = "Fine (k=10)") )  ggplot(comp_df, aes(x = Factor, fill = Category)) +   geom_bar(width = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~ Level, scales = "free_x") +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(title = "Deep NMF: Coarse vs. Fine Factor Assignments",        subtitle = "Coarse factors separate AML/Control; fine factors resolve subtypes",        y = "Samples") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Factorization Graphs
text: second layer’s H2H_2 matrix (3 × 10) reveals fine factors compose coarse ones:  coarse factor draws distinct subset fine factors — interpretable bridge resolutions.
code: H2 <- deep$coarse$H  # 3 x 10 d2 <- deep$coarse$d  # Normalize for visualization H2_scaled <- sweep(H2, 1, d2, "*") h_max <- max(abs(H2_scaled)) H2_norm <- H2_scaled / h_max  conn_df <- expand.grid(Coarse = paste0("C", 1:3), Fine = paste0("F", 1:10)) conn_df$Weight <- as.vector(H2_norm)  ggplot(conn_df, aes(x = Fine, y = Coarse, fill = Weight)) +   geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "darkgreen") +   labs(title = "Deep NMF: How Fine Factors Compose into Coarse Ones",        subtitle = "H₂ matrix connecting layer 2 (coarse) to layer 1 (fine) factors",        fill = "Relative\nWeight") +   theme_minimal()

title: Factorization Graphs
text: Guided NMF vignette covers target regularization detail. Within factor graph, apply W() H() per-factor config objects: target biases HH toward island-specific centroids optimization, producing embedding better class separability unsupervised NMF. Use positive target_lambda enrichment (enhance structure) negative removal (suppress label-correlated variation, useful batch correction). /within class variance ratio (higher = better separated) See Guided NMF refine(), compute_target(), classification benchmarks.
code: data(hawaiibirds) meta_h <- attr(hawaiibirds, "metadata_h")  # Fit a baseline model, then derive targets from island labels baseline <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 8, seed = 42, maxit = 50) target <- compute_target(baseline@h, factor(meta_h$island))  # Graph with target regularization on H inp <- factor_input(hawaiibirds, name = "birds") guided <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 8, name = "guided",                     H = H(target = target, target_lambda = 0.3)) net <- factor_net(inp, guided, factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42)) guided_result <- fit(net) H_unsup  <- baseline@h H_guided <- guided_result$guided$H island   <- factor(meta_h$island)  # Measure class separability: ratio of between-class to within-class variance separability <- function(H, labels) {   grand_mean <- rowMeans(H)   levels_l <- levels(labels)   k <- nrow(H)   between_var <- 0   within_var  <- 0   for (lev in levels_l) {     idx <- which(labels == lev)     n_l <- length(idx)     class_mean <- rowMeans(H[, idx, drop = FALSE])     between_var <- between_var + n_l * sum((class_mean - grand_mean)^2)     within_var  <- within_var + sum((H[, idx] - class_mean)^2)   }   between_var / max(within_var, .Machine$double.eps) }  sep_df <- data.frame(   Method = c("Unsupervised", "Target-regularized (λ=0.3)"),   Separability = c(separability(H_unsup, island),                    separability(H_guided, island)) ) knitr::kable(sep_df, digits = 3,              caption = "Between/within class variance ratio (higher = better separated)")

title: Factorization Graphs
text: cross_validate_graph() automates hyperparameter search. provide function builds output layer parameter list, grid values search: Top parameter combinations mean test loss  works graph architecture — cross-validate second-layer rank deep NMF, regularization strength shared factorization, parameter exposed layer_fn.
code: data(aml) inp <- factor_input(aml, name = "aml")  layer_fn <- function(p) nmf_layer(inp, k = p$k, L1 = p$L1, name = "cv") params <- list(k = c(3, 5, 8, 12), L1 = c(0, 0.01, 0.1))  config <- factor_config(test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 42, maxit = 50) cv <- cross_validate_graph(inp, layer_fn, params, config,                            reps = 2, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE)  knitr::kable(head(cv$summary, 6), digits = 5,              caption = "Top parameter combinations by mean test loss") cv_df <- cv$summary cv_df$L1 <- factor(cv_df$L1) cv_df$k <- as.numeric(cv_df$k)  ggplot(cv_df, aes(x = k, y = mean_test_loss, color = L1, group = L1)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(size = 2.5) +   geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_test_loss - se_test_loss,                     ymax = mean_test_loss + se_test_loss),                 width = 0.3, linewidth = 0.5) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(title = "Cross-Validation: Rank × L1 Grid Search",        x = "Rank (k)", y = "Mean Test Loss ± SE", color = "L1") +   theme_minimal()

title: Factorization Graphs
text: real power composability. block node; wire freely: Shared + guided — two modalities label guidance: Deep + CV — search second-layer rank: Branching + concatenation — parallel factorizations different ranks, merged:
code: inp1 <- factor_input(A1, name = "rna") inp2 <- factor_input(A2, name = "protein") layer <- nmf_layer(factor_shared(inp1, inp2), k = 10, name = "joint",                    H = H(target = T_mat, target_lambda = 0.3)) net <- factor_net(list(inp1, inp2), layer, factor_config(maxit = 100)) layer1 <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 20, name = "fine") cv <- cross_validate_graph(   inp,   layer_fn = function(p) nmf_layer(layer1, k = p$k, name = "coarse"),   params = list(k = c(3, 5, 8)),   config = factor_config(test_fraction = 0.1, maxit = 50) ) branch_lo <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 3, name = "coarse") branch_hi <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 10, name = "fine") merged <- nmf_layer(factor_concat(branch_lo, branch_hi), k = 5, name = "merged") net <- factor_net(inp, merged, factor_config(maxit = 50))

title: Getting Started with RcppML
text: RcppML decomposes nonnegative matrix AA low-rank factors ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H, WW captures features, HH captures sample loadings, dd gives per-factor scale. Built Rcpp Eigen OpenMP parallelism optional CUDA GPU support, RcppML designed large sparse matrices common genomics, recommender systems, image analysis. Key capabilities include: NMF coordinate-descent Cholesky NNLS solvers SVD / PCA optional non-negativity L1 constraints Cross-validation via speckled holdout automatic rank selection Distribution-aware losses: Gaussian, Poisson, Generalized Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie — plus zero-inflation Regularization: L1, L2, L21, angular, graph Laplacian, upper bounds Consensus clustering divisive clustering (dclust) Composable factorization graphs via factor_net() StreamPress .spz format --core computation GPU acceleration via CUDA

title: Getting Started with RcppML
text: Install CRAN: install development version: GPU support, see GPU Acceleration vignette.
code: install.packages("RcppML") remotes::install_github("zdebruine/RcppML")

title: Getting Started with RcppML

code: data(hawaiibirds) model <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 10, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100)

title: Getting Started with RcppML
text: can also decompose data SVD, captures directions maximum variance rather additive nonneg parts:  first component captures dominant species-composition gradient, subsequent component adds progressively less explanatory power — hallmark effective dimensionality reduction. PCA (centered optionally scaled SVD), use pca() convenience wrapper: PCA centers data decomposition, first component captures dominant direction variation rather overall mean level. Use scale = TRUE data features different units widely varying magnitude.
code: data(hawaiibirds) sv <- svd(hawaiibirds, k = 10) pc <- pca(hawaiibirds, k = 5, seed = 42)  # center = TRUE by default # Equivalent to: svd(hawaiibirds, k = 5, center = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with RcppML
text: Use speckled holdout cross-validation find best rank AML data:  Training loss always decreases rank, test loss reveals model begins overfit. test-loss minimum identifies optimal number factors.
code: data(aml) cv <- nmf(aml, k = c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10), test_fraction = 0.2, seed = 42)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: scale, dimensionality reduction bottlenecked dense linear algebra. NMF iteration computes Gram matrices (HHTH H^T, WTWW^T W) right-hand-side products (AHTA H^T, WTAW^T ) — operations map directly onto GPU hardware thousands cores execute matrix multiplies parallel. RcppML’s GPU backend uses NVIDIA CUDA cuBLAS cuSPARSE accelerate operations, achieving 10–40× speedups real single-cell datasets even fully-utilized 56-thread CPU. GPU advantage uniform across operations. Standard NMF already benefits significantly, cross-validation SVD randomized show largest gains modes perform dense BLAS work (Gram matrix corrections, block matrix multiplies) maps directly onto GPU hardware. GPU optional — code falls back CPU transparently CUDA unavailable.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: resource parameter accepts "gpu", "cpu", "auto" (default). "auto", RcppML uses GPU available falls back CPU otherwise.
code: library(RcppML)  # Check GPU availability gpu_available() #> [1] TRUE gpu_info() #>   device              name total_mb free_mb #> 1      0  NVIDIA H100 NVL    95830   93500  # NMF with GPU — same API, add resource = "gpu" model <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "gpu", seed = 42)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: much faster GPU practice? benchmarked 10 operations spanning standard NMF, cross-validation, IRLS (KL divergence), sparse-mask NMF, SVD two real single-cell RNA-seq datasets.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
code: library(RcppML) library(Matrix) library(SeuratData) library(Seurat)  # ── hcabm40k: 40,000 bone marrow cells ── data("hcabm40k") hca <- UpdateSeuratObject(hcabm40k) counts <- hca[["RNA"]]$counts                    # 17,369 genes × 40,000 cells  # Select top 5,000 variable genes, log-normalize gene_vars <- Matrix::rowMeans(counts^2) - Matrix::rowMeans(counts)^2 top5k <- head(order(gene_vars, decreasing = TRUE), 5000) hca_5k <- counts[top5k, ] lib_sizes <- Matrix::colSums(hca_5k) hca_norm <- log1p(hca_5k %*% Diagonal(x = 1e4 / lib_sizes)) hca_norm <- as(hca_norm, "dgCMatrix") # 5,000 × 40,000, nnz ≈ 33M, density ≈ 16.5%  # 10K-cell subset for expensive k = 64 operations set.seed(42) hca_10k <- hca_norm[, sample(ncol(hca_norm), 10000)] # 5,000 × 10,000, nnz ≈ 8.3M  # ── pbmc3k: 500 PBMCs (bundled with RcppML) ── data(pbmc3k, package = "RcppML") tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, tmp) pbmc <- st_read(tmp) pbmc_norm <- log1p(pbmc %*% Diagonal(x = 1e4 / Matrix::colSums(pbmc))) pbmc_norm <- as(pbmc_norm, "dgCMatrix") # 8,000 × 500, nnz ≈ 412K

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: benchmarks ran NVIDIA H100 NVL (96 GB HBM3) 56-thread Intel Xeon Gold 6238R (2 × 28 cores, threads active). NMF iterations forced tol = 0 maxit = 20. CPU (56-thread Xeon) vs. GPU (H100 NVL) benchmarks

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Three patterns stand : SVD randomized shows largest speedup (43×). Randomized SVD performs full matrix–dense block multiplies (AQA Q, ATBA^T B) translate handful cuSPARSE/cuBLAS calls GPU. Cross-validation NMF high rank close behind (32×) CV corrects Gram matrix every held-entry iteration — O(k2)O(k^2) update per test-set column batches naturally GPU. operations achieve ≥ 10× speedup, even fully-utilized 56-thread Xeon. NMF high rank (k=64k = 64, 33×), IRLS KL-divergence (12×), sparse-mask NMF (14×) benefit GPU BLAS acceleration. Speedups scale problem size rank. 40K-cell NMF k=20k = 20 (14×) benefits less k=64k = 64 10K cells (33×) higher rank increases Gram matrix NNLS work GPU absorbs efficiently. fewer CPU threads (e.g., 8), speedups increase proportionally.

what: Why GPU Gains Are Larger for CV and IRLS
title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Standard NMF spends iteration two operations: HHT(k×k),AHT(m×k)H H^T \quad (k \times k), \qquad H^T \quad (m \times k) Cross-validation adds correction step: held-column jj test set, Gram matrix adjusted subtracting contribution missing entries: Gj=HHT−∑∈mask(j)hihiTG_j = H H^T - \sum_{\\text{mask}(j)} h_i h_i^T CPU, per-column correction loop sequential. GPU, corrections test columns batched single sparse outer-product kernel. result: CV overhead dominates CPU time becomes negligible GPU, pushing CV speedups well standard NMF speedups. IRLS losses (KL divergence, Gamma–Poisson, etc.) similarly increase per-iteration work solving weighted NNLS subproblems require additional Gram matrix updates observation-specific weights — , heavy BLAS work GPU absorbs minimal overhead.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: compute backend determined (highest priority first): resource parameter nmf() svd() RCPPML_RESOURCE environment variable options(RcppML.gpu = TRUE/FALSE) Auto-detection (GPU available, else CPU)
code: # Force CPU even when GPU is available model <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "cpu", seed = 42)  # Session-wide override via environment variable Sys.setenv(RCPPML_RESOURCE = "cpu")  # Or via R option options(RcppML.gpu = FALSE)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU-unsupported feature requested, RcppML falls back CPU automatically error.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: large datasets stored .spz files, st_read_gpu() transfers matrix directly GPU memory, avoiding full CPU-side dgCMatrix copy: See StreamPress vignette .spz format details.
code: gpu_handle <- st_read_gpu("large_dataset.spz") model <- nmf(gpu_handle, k = 30, seed = 42) st_free_gpu(gpu_handle)   # optional — GC handles cleanup
