title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: NVIDIA GPU compute capability ≥\geq 7.0 (Volta newer) CUDA Toolkit ≥\geq 12.x (cuBLAS cuSPARSE included)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Adapt -gencode flags GPU architecture: CUDA architecture flags GPU family
code: # 1. Load CUDA on your system module load cuda/12.8.1   # HPC example  # 2. Build the GPU shared library (use the project Makefile) cd RcppML/src/ make -f Makefile.gpu install  # Or manually: nvcc -O3 \   -gencode arch=compute_80,code=sm_80 \   -gencode arch=compute_90,code=sm_90 \   -std=c++17 -Xcompiler -fPIC --shared \   -I../inst/include \   -o ../inst/lib/RcppML_gpu.so \   gpu_bridge_nmf.cu gpu_bridge_svd.cu gpu_bridge_cluster.cu \   gpu_bridge_utils.cu sp_gpu_bridge.cu \   -lcublas -lcusparse -lcuda  # 3. Verify Rscript -e 'library(RcppML); cat("GPU:", gpu_available(), "\n")' #> GPU: TRUE

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: RcppML searches RcppML_gpu.order: inst/lib/ → src/ → package lib/ → working directory root.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: NMF GPU memory usage approximately: Memory≈nnz×12+(m+n)×k×8 bytes\text{Memory} \approx \text{nnz} \times 12 + (m + n) \times k \times 8 \text{ bytes} nnz number non-zeros, mm nn matrix dimensions, kk rank. 30,000 × 20,000 matrix 5% density k=30k = 30, roughly 3.6 GB — well within 32 GB GPU.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
code: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH --partition=gpu #SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8 #SBATCH --mem=32G #SBATCH --time=04:00:00  module load r/4.5.2 cuda/12.8.1 Rscript my_analysis.R

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU detected? Common causes: CUDA drivers missing outdated (nvidia-smi check), GPU compute capability < 7.0, RcppML_gpu.search path, architecture mismatch (library compiled sm_90 won’t load V100). Precision: GPU uses FP32 core operations FP64 selectively numerically sensitive accumulations. Results equivalent CPU (FP64) within floating-point tolerance.
code: gpu_available(force_recheck = TRUE) #> [1] FALSE

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: API, different backend: resource = "gpu" change needed. 10–40× speedups real single-cell data fully-utilized 56-thread CPU, SVD randomized cross-validation showing largest gains. fewer CPU threads, speedups scale proportionally. Automatic fallback: GPU unavailable feature CPU-, RcppML falls back silently. Direct disk--GPU via st_read_gpu() large .spz datasets. See NMF Fundamentals vignette comprehensive parameter documentation StreamPress vignette .spz file format details.

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: NMF discovers latent structure data alone — labels needed. class labels (cell types, tissue origins, experimental conditions), can steer factorization produce embeddings better separate known groups without collapsing information toward centroids. RcppML provides three complementary mechanisms: Post-hoc refinement via refine() — correct existing NMF embedding using label information -NMF target regularization via compute_target() + target_H/target_lambda parameters — bias factorization toward (away ) class-specific structure optimization Information removal via negative target_lambda — suppress target-correlated structure using PROJ_ADV (Projected Adversarial Removal)

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: hawaiibirds dataset contains bird species counts across 1,183 geographic grid cells 8 Hawaiian islands. ’ll use island origin class label.
code: data(hawaiibirds) A <- hawaiibirds meta <- attr(hawaiibirds, "metadata_h") labels <- meta$island cat("Data:", nrow(A), "species x", ncol(A), "grid cells,",     nlevels(labels), "islands\n") #> Data: 183 species x 1183 grid cells, 8 islands

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: First, run NMF label guidance: Evaluate embedding using assess(), computes clustering quality (NMI, ARI), classification accuracy (KNN, logistic regression, random forest), silhouette scores:
code:
set.seed(42) model_base <- nmf(A, k = 8, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100) res_base <- assess(model_base, labels, classifiers = c("knn", "lr")) cat("Baseline NMI:", round(res_base$metrics$nmi, 3), "
") #> Baseline NMI: 0.488 cat("Baseline ARI:", round(res_base$metrics$ari, 3), "
") #> Baseline ARI: 0.382 cat("Baseline KNN accuracy:", round(res_base$metrics$accuracy_knn, 3), "
") #> Baseline KNN accuracy: 0.898

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: refine() applies OAS-ZCA whitened centroid correction existing embedding. shifts sample toward class centroid decorrelated space, improving class separation without distorting overall structure. Internally, refine() Frobenius-normalizes target match energy H, lambda interpretable fraction H-energy added correction:
code:
model_refined <- refine(model_base, labels = labels, lambda = 0.5) res_refined <- assess(model_refined, labels, classifiers = c("knn", "lr")) cat("Refined NMI:", round(res_refined$metrics$nmi, 3), "
") #> Refined NMI: 0.827 cat("Refined ARI:", round(res_refined$metrics$ari, 3), "
") #> Refined ARI: 0.669 cat("Refined KNN accuracy:", round(res_refined$metrics$accuracy_knn, 3), "
") #> Refined KNN accuracy: 0.994

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: Instead correcting fact, can bias NMF optimization toward class-specific structure. compute_target() builds target matrix labels, target_H/target_lambda inject regularization term: target regularization modifies NNLS subproblem adding |λ|⋅|\lambda| \cdot Gram matrix diagonal λ⋅T\lambda \cdot T right-hand side, gently pulling H toward target structure every iteration. Note: target_lambda operates scale NNLS system (Gram matrix), small values (0.05–0.15) typically produce large effects.
code:
T_mat <- compute_target(model_base@h, labels) set.seed(42) model_target <- nmf(A, k = 8, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100,                     target_H = T_mat, target_lambda = 0.10) res_target <- assess(model_target, labels, classifiers = c("knn", "lr")) cat("Target reg NMI:", round(res_target$metrics$nmi, 3), "
") #> Target reg NMI: 0.846 cat("Target reg ARI:", round(res_target$metrics$ari, 3), "
") #> Target reg ARI: 0.663 cat("Target reg KNN accuracy:", round(res_target$metrics$accuracy_knn, 3), "
") #> Target reg KNN accuracy: 0.997

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
code: mse <- function(m) {   resid <- as.matrix(A) - m@w %*% diag(m@d) %*% m@h   mean(resid^2) } mse_base <- mse(model_base) results <- data.frame(   Method = c("Baseline", "Post-hoc refine (λ=0.5)",              "Target regularization (λ=0.10)"),   NMI = c(res_base$metrics$nmi, res_refined$metrics$nmi,           res_target$metrics$nmi),   ARI = c(res_base$metrics$ari, res_refined$metrics$ari,           res_target$metrics$ari),   KNN_acc = c(res_base$metrics$accuracy_knn, res_refined$metrics$accuracy_knn,               res_target$metrics$accuracy_knn),   MSE_ratio = c(1.0, mse(model_refined) / mse_base,                 mse(model_target) / mse_base) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 3) results[, 5] <- round(results[, 5], 1) knitr::kable(results, row.names = FALSE,              col.names = c("Method", "NMI", "ARI", "KNN Accuracy",                            "MSE ratio"))

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: UMAPs H embeddings show method affects island separation:  baseline embedding shows partial separation improves enrichment methods. Target regularization integrates label information directly optimization.
code: umap_df <- rbind(   make_umap_df(model_base, labels, "Baseline"),   make_umap_df(model_refined, labels, "Post-hoc refine"),   make_umap_df(model_target, labels, "Target regularization") ) umap_df$Method <- factor(umap_df$Method,   levels = c("Baseline", "Post-hoc refine",              "Target regularization"))  ggplot(umap_df, aes(UMAP1, UMAP2, color = Island)) +   geom_point(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~Method, scales = "free") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3, alpha = 1)))

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: factor distribute across islands? Stacked bar plots reveal whether factors island-specific shared. model, normalize H columns unit sum, average within island:  baseline, factors spread diffusely across islands. target regularization, factor becomes specific one islands — regularization encourages factor specialization.
code: make_bar_df <- function(model, labels, method) {   H <- model@h   # Normalize each column (sample) to proportions   H_prop <- sweep(H, 2, colSums(H), "/")   # Average proportions within each island   islands <- levels(labels)   k <- nrow(H)   avg <- matrix(0, k, length(islands), dimnames = list(     paste0("F", seq_len(k)), islands))   for (i in seq_along(islands)) {     idx <- labels == islands[i]     avg[, i] <- rowMeans(H_prop[, idx, drop = FALSE])   }   df <- as.data.frame(as.table(avg))   names(df) <- c("Factor", "Island", "Proportion")   df$Method <- method   df }  bar_df <- rbind(   make_bar_df(model_base, labels, "Baseline"),   make_bar_df(model_refined, labels, "Post-hoc refine"),   make_bar_df(model_target, labels, "Target regularization") ) bar_df$Method <- factor(bar_df$Method,   levels = c("Baseline", "Post-hoc refine",              "Target regularization"))  ggplot(bar_df, aes(Island, Proportion, fill = Factor)) +   geom_col(position = "stack") +   facet_wrap(~Method) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(y = "Mean factor proportion")

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: Lambda tuning: Start target_lambda = 0.10 -NMF regularization, lambda = 0.5 post-hoc refinement. Values 0.15 -NMF can saturate strongly-structured data. Monitor MSE ratio (reconstruction cost / baseline): ratios 3x suggest -regularization.

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: Sometimes want opposite: embedding invariant known grouping variable. instance, island origin represents batch effect confound, may want factors capture ecological patterns across islands rather island-specific ones. RcppML uses PROJ_ADV (Projected Adversarial Removal) suppress target-correlated structure target_lambda negative. PROJ_ADV modifies NNLS Gram matrix deflate directions aligned target: Compute target Gram matrix: GT=TT⊤/nG_T = T T^\top / n Trace-scale match reconstruction Gram: α=tr(G)/tr(GT)\alpha = \mathrm{tr}(G) / \mathrm{tr}(G_T) Subtract scaled target Gram: G̃=G−|λ|⋅α⋅GT\tilde{G} = G - |\lambda| \cdot \alpha \cdot G_T Eigendecompose G̃=VΛV⊤\tilde{G} = V \Lambda V^\top clip negative eigenvalues ε=10−8\varepsilon = 10^{-8} Reconstruct: G̃←Vmax(Λ,ε)V⊤\tilde{G} \leftarrow V \max(\Lambda, \varepsilon) V^\top trace-scaling step 2 ensures λ\lambda interpretable fraction target energy remove, regardless absolute scale G GTG_T. eigenvalue clipping step 4 keeps modified Gram positive-definite, directions aligned target suppressed (eigenvalues shrink get clipped) orthogonal directions preserved. right-hand side (b) modified.
code:
set.seed(42) model_remove <- nmf(A, k = 8, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100,                     target_H = T_mat, target_lambda = -0.10) res_remove <- assess(model_remove, labels, classifiers = c("knn", "lr")) cat("Island-removed NMI:", round(res_remove$metrics$nmi, 3), "
") #> Island-removed NMI: 0.257 cat("Island-removed ARI:", round(res_remove$metrics$ari, 3), "
") #> Island-removed ARI: 0.156 cat("Island-removed KNN accuracy:", round(res_remove$metrics$accuracy_knn, 3), "
") #> Island-removed KNN accuracy: 0.829 cat("Island-removed MSE ratio:", round(mse(model_remove) / mse_base, 1), "x
") #> Island-removed MSE ratio: 3.6 x

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
code: results_all <- data.frame(   Method = c("Baseline", "Target enrichment (\u03bb=0.10)",              "Target removal (\u03bb=\u22120.10)"),   NMI = c(res_base$metrics$nmi, res_target$metrics$nmi,           res_remove$metrics$nmi),   ARI = c(res_base$metrics$ari, res_target$metrics$ari,           res_remove$metrics$ari),   KNN_acc = c(res_base$metrics$accuracy_knn, res_target$metrics$accuracy_knn,               res_remove$metrics$accuracy_knn),   MSE_ratio = c(1.0, mse(model_target) / mse_base,                 mse(model_remove) / mse_base) ) results_all[, 2:4] <- round(results_all[, 2:4], 3) results_all[, 5] <- round(results_all[, 5], 1) knitr::kable(results_all, row.names = FALSE,              col.names = c("Method", "NMI", "ARI", "KNN Accuracy",                            "MSE ratio"))

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: Enrichment sharpens island clusters; removal mixes together. remaining structure removal UMAP reflects ecological signal explained island identity (e.g., elevation, habitat type, shared species assemblages).
code: umap_remove_df <- rbind(   make_umap_df(model_base, labels, "Baseline"),   make_umap_df(model_target, labels, "Enrichment (λ=0.10)"),   make_umap_df(model_remove, labels, "Removal (λ=−0.10)") ) umap_remove_df$Method <- factor(umap_remove_df$Method,   levels = c("Baseline", "Enrichment (λ=0.10)", "Removal (λ=−0.10)"))  ggplot(umap_remove_df, aes(UMAP1, UMAP2, color = Island)) +   geom_point(size = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~Method, scales = "free") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3, alpha = 1)))

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: negative lambda, PROJ_ADV suppresses target-correlated directions Gram matrix. Factors become uniformly distributed across islands — factor captures cross-island patterns rather island-specific signal. useful : Batch correction: Removing batch-specific variation preserving biological signal Confound removal: Factoring known confounders (e.g., sequencing depth, donor effects) Integration: Creating shared embedding across experimental conditions
code: bar_remove_df <- rbind(   make_bar_df(model_base, labels, "Baseline"),   make_bar_df(model_target, labels, "Enrichment (λ=0.10)"),   make_bar_df(model_remove, labels, "Removal (λ=−0.10)") ) bar_remove_df$Method <- factor(bar_remove_df$Method,   levels = c("Baseline", "Enrichment (λ=0.10)", "Removal (λ=−0.10)"))  ggplot(bar_remove_df, aes(Island, Proportion, fill = Factor)) +   geom_col(position = "stack") +   facet_wrap(~Method) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(y = "Mean factor proportion")

title: Guided NMF: Label-Informed Factorization
text: Target regularization works factor graph API via W() H() per-factor configs:
code: T_mat <- compute_target(model_base@h, labels)  # Enrichment net_enrich <- factor_input(A) |>   nmf_layer(k = 8, H = H(target = T_mat, target_lambda = 0.10)) |>   factor_net()  # Removal net_remove <- factor_input(A) |>   nmf_layer(k = 8, H = H(target = T_mat, target_lambda = -0.10)) |>   factor_net()

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: can decompose nonnegative matrix interpretable parts? Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) answers finding additive, parts-based representations: factor captures distinct pattern, weights nonneg, factors combine constructively. Unlike PCA, components can cancel , NMF factors always additive — making directly interpretable “parts” data. NMF widely used topic modeling, image decomposition, gene expression analysis, recommendation systems. RcppML provides high-performance implementation distinctive ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H factorization, diagonal scaling dd makes factors comparable ordered importance.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Key parameters: Advanced parameters passed via ...: Initialization options (passed via seed): Normalization options: Normalization regularization interaction: L1 regularization applied normalization iteration. norm = "L1", factor columns rescaled sum 1 L1 soft-thresholding. means effective penalty depends normalization mode; L1 value produces different sparsity levels L1 vs. L2 normalization. Start norm = "L1" (default) using L1 regularization.
code: nmf(data, k, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100, seed = NULL,     L1 = c(0, 0), L2 = c(0, 0),     mask = NULL, loss = "mse",     nonneg = c(TRUE, TRUE),     verbose = FALSE, ...)

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: nmf() function returns S4 object class nmf slots accessed via $: model@w model$w — m×km \times k feature loading matrix (W) model@h model$h — k×nk \times n sample embedding matrix (H) model@d model$d — length-kk diagonal scaling vector model@misc — metadata list (iterations, runtime, loss, etc.) dim(model) — returns c(m, n, k) head(model, n) — first nn factors model[] — subset specific factor indices canonical S4 accessor @ (e.g., model@w). $ shorthand also works due method dispatch.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: prod(model) — reconstructs W⋅diag(d)⋅HW \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H (dense matrix) evaluate(model, data) — reconstruction loss (defaults MSE regardless loss used fitting; specify evaluate(model, data, loss = "gp") compute loss distribution used fitting) mse(model$w, model$d, model$h, data) — standalone MSE computation

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: align(model, ref) — reorder factors match reference model (Hungarian algorithm) sparsity(model) — fraction near-zero entries W H cosine(x, y) — column-wise cosine similarity matrices bipartiteMatch(cost_matrix) — Hungarian algorithm optimal factor pairing (returns 0-indexed $assignment)

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: tol controls convergence threshold. model converges maximum change factor (measured correlation distance) falls tol. maxit provides hard iteration cap. on_iteration accepts callback function(iter, train_loss, test_loss) called iteration.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: NMF solves constrained optimization problem: minW≥0,H≥0∥−W⋅diag(d)⋅H∥F2\min_{W \geq 0,\, H \geq 0} \|- W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H\|_F^2 AA m×nm \times n nonneg matrix, WW m×km \times k, HH k×nk \times n, dd length-kk scaling vector.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: RcppML uses alternating non-negative least squares: fix H, solve W; fix W, solve H. full iteration, columns W rows H normalized scales absorbed dd. diagonal scaling makes factors directly comparable ordered importance sort_model = TRUE.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Random initialization gives noisy starting point may require iterations. SVD-based methods (Lanczos, IRLBA) compute truncated SVD warm start, typically converging faster — especially well-conditioned data.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: NMF solutions unique: different seeds lead different local optima. Always set seed reproducibility. robust factorizations across multiple random starts, use consensus_nmf() (see Clustering vignette).

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: standard NMF, W H solved independently alternating updates. Projective NMF adds structural constraint: instead solving H, computed linear projection data W: H=diag(d)⋅WT⋅AH = \text{diag}(d) \cdot W^T \cdot means H entirely determined W — free parameters H. Enable projective = TRUE: use projective NMF? orthogonal features: projection constraint forces W columns nearly orthogonal, way minimize reconstruction error H=WTAH = W^T columns W capture non-overlapping parts data. Fewer degrees freedom: Since H determined W, model roughly half many free parameters, acting implicit regularizer. Unique H given W: Eliminates one source non-uniqueness factorization. tradeoff higher reconstruction error — model constrained, fit data closely. Projective NMF useful want clearly separable, non-overlapping factors interpretability rather minimum reconstruction error. Standard vs. projective NMF AML data (k = 6). Lower W orthogonality = independent factors. Projective NMF yields W columns substantially orthogonal (lower -diagonal cosine), making factor correspond distinct set features. comes cost reconstruction fidelity — projection constraint prevents model fitting data tightly. Note: projective = TRUE symmetric = TRUE combined. symmetric matrices (=ATA = ^T), symmetric NMF enforces H=WTH = W^T directly.
code: model <- nmf(data, k = 6, projective = TRUE) data(aml) m_std  <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5) m_proj <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, projective = TRUE)  # Orthogonality: mean absolute off-diagonal cosine between W columns # (lower = more orthogonal) ortho_metric <- function(mat) {   cs <- cosine(mat, mat)   diag(cs) <- 0   mean(abs(cs)) }  knitr::kable(data.frame(   Mode = c("Standard", "Projective"),   `W orthogonality` = round(c(ortho_metric(m_std@w), ortho_metric(m_proj@w)), 3),   `W mean sparsity` = round(c(mean(sparsity(m_std)$sparsity[1:6]),                                mean(sparsity(m_proj)$sparsity[1:6])), 3),   `Reconstruction MSE` = format(c(evaluate(m_std, aml), evaluate(m_proj, aml)),                                  digits = 3, scientific = TRUE),   check.names = FALSE ), caption = "Standard vs. projective NMF on AML data (k = 6). Lower W orthogonality = more independent factors.")

what: Example 1: Recovering Known Factors from Synthetic Data
title: NMF Fundamentals
text: generate synthetic matrix 5 true factors test whether NMF can recover . compare learned factors ground truth, compute cosine similarity pair W columns. bipartiteMatch() function finds optimal one--one assignment (note: returns 0-indexed assignments): Per-factor cosine similarity learned true W columns. Factor recovery quality depends signal--noise ratio, matrix size, degree overlap true factors. use noise = 3.0, meaning noise amplitude three times signal — challenging setting. Despite , NMF recovers five factors high cosine similarity, demonstrating robustness additive noise.  left panel shows factor loadings recovered projecting noisy data matrix onto true H — noise substantially degrades block structure. right panel shows NMF’s learned W, block-diagonal structure clearly recovered. NMF acts denoiser: jointly optimizing W H, separates signal noise effectively direct projection.  scatter plots compare noisy input loadings (x-axis) NMF-recovered loadings (y-axis) factor. NMF sharpens loadings — points pulled toward axes, reflecting non-negativity constraint’s tendency suppress noise produce sparser, interpretable factors. cosine similarity panel title measures agreement true (clean) factors.
code: sim <- simulateNMF(200, 80, k = 5, noise = 3.0, seed = 42) model <- nmf(sim$A, k = 5, seed = 1, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100) sim_cos <- cosine(model$w, sim$w) match_result <- bipartiteMatch(1 - sim_cos + 1e-10) assignment <- match_result$assignment + 1L  # convert to 1-indexed  per_factor_cos <- sapply(seq_len(5), function(i) {   sim_cos[i, assignment[i]] })  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Factor = 1:5,     `Cosine Similarity` = round(per_factor_cos, 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Per-factor cosine similarity between learned and true W columns." ) # Align learned W to true W using bipartite matching learned_w <- model$w  # Compute noisy W: least-squares projection of A onto true H (shows noise corruption) noisy_w <- as.matrix(sim$A %*% t(sim$h) %*% solve(sim$h %*% t(sim$h))) noisy_w <- noisy_w[, assignment]  # L1-normalize columns so both heatmaps share the same [0, 1] scale l1_norm <- function(mat) sweep(mat, 2, colSums(abs(mat)), "/") noisy_w   <- l1_norm(noisy_w) learned_w <- l1_norm(learned_w)  # Hierarchical clustering on the combined matrix for consistent row/col order combined_w <- cbind(noisy_w, learned_w) row_hc <- hclust(dist(combined_w)) row_ord <- row_hc$order col_hc <- hclust(dist(t(noisy_w))) col_ord <- col_hc$order  noisy_w <- noisy_w[row_ord, col_ord] learned_w <- learned_w[row_ord, col_ord]  # Build long-format data for ggplot make_heatmap_df <- function(mat, label) {   df <- expand.grid(Feature = seq_len(nrow(mat)), Factor = seq_len(ncol(mat)))   df$Value <- as.vector(mat)   df$Source <- label   df } hm_df <- rbind(   make_heatmap_df(noisy_w, "W from Noisy Data"),   make_heatmap_df(learned_w, "NMF Learned W") ) hm_df$Source <- factor(hm_df$Source, levels = c("W from Noisy Data", "NMF Learned W"))  ggplot(hm_df, aes(x = Factor, y = Feature, fill = Value)) +   geom_raster() +   facet_wrap(~Source) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +   labs(title = "Noisy Input vs. NMF Recovery",        subtitle = "Columns L1-normalized; NMF denoises the factor loadings",        x = "Factor", y = "Feature") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(aspect.ratio = 1,         axis.text.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank()) # Per-factor scatter plots: noisy vs. learned W column values scatter_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:5, function(i) {   data.frame(     Noisy = noisy_w[, i],     Learned = learned_w[, i],     Factor = paste0("Factor ", i, " (cos=", round(per_factor_cos[i], 2), ")")   ) }))  ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x = Noisy, y = Learned)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8, color = "steelblue") +   geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +   facet_wrap(~ Factor, nrow = 2) +   coord_fixed() +   labs(title = "Per-Factor Comparison: Noisy Input vs. NMF Learned W",        x = "Noisy W loading", y = "NMF W loading") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Example 1, knew true rank k=5k = 5 generated data. practice, correct rank unknown. Cross-validation provides principled answer: hold random fraction matrix entries, fit NMF remainder, measure prediction error held-set. rank minimizes test error best choice.  plot reveals classic bias–variance tradeoff: Underfitting (k<5k < 5): training test MSE high. model lacks capacity capture five true factors, represent data’s structure performs poorly seen unseen entries. Optimal rank (k=5k = 5): Test MSE reaches minimum. model exactly enough factors capture true structure without fitting noise. Overfitting (k>5k > 5): Training MSE continues decrease — model fits training entries ever closely — test MSE increases. Extra factors fit noise training set generalize held-entries. works: nmf(data, k = 1:15, test_fraction = 0.2) randomly masks 20% matrix entries fitting. iteration model trained visible 80%; masked entries predicted current W⋅diag(d)⋅HW \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H scored true values. rank lowest test MSE selected.
code: cv <- nmf(sim$A, k = 2:15, test_fraction = 0.05, cv_seed = 1:3,           tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200) agg <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv, FUN = mean) best_k <- agg$k[which.min(agg$test_mse)] plot(cv) +   geom_vline(xintercept = best_k, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +   annotate("text", x = best_k + 0.3, y = max(agg$test_mse) * 0.98,            label = paste("optimal k =", best_k), hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +   labs(     title = "Cross-Validation for Rank Selection",     subtitle = "Test MSE identifies the correct rank; training MSE always decreases"   ) +   theme_minimal()

previous_headings: Worked Examples > Example 2: Choosing Rank with Cross-Validation
title: NMF Fundamentals
text: cross-rank plot shows kk best. Now look inside individual fits see training test loss evolve iteration iteration. reveals dynamics overfitting real time.  Three distinct behaviors emerge: k = 2 (underfitting): training test loss decrease smoothly converge together. However, plateau level well optimal test error — model simply represent five factors two components. k = 5 (optimal): losses decrease rapidly converge near lowest achievable test error. Training test losses track closely, indicating model generalizes well. k = 15 (overfitting): Training loss continues fall, test loss reaches minimum early increases model iterates . excess factors memorize training-set noise expense held-prediction. combination cross-rank plot (kk choose) per-iteration traces (choice matters) provides complete picture rank selection NMF.
code: iter_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(2, 5, 15), function(k) {   m <- nmf(sim$A, k = k, seed = 1, tol = 1e-10, maxit = 100, test_fraction = 0.05, resource = "cpu")   n <- length(m@misc$loss_history)   data.frame(     Iteration = rep(1:n, 2),     MSE = c(m@misc$loss_history, m@misc$test_loss_history),     Set = rep(c("Train", "Test"), each = n),     Rank = factor(paste0("k = ", k), levels = paste0("k = ", c(2, 5, 15)))   ) })) ggplot(iter_data, aes(x = Iteration, y = MSE, color = Set)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~ Rank, scales = "free_x") +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Train" = "#377EB8", "Test" = "#E41A1C")) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) sprintf("%.1e", x)) +   labs(     title = "Per-Iteration Loss: Underfitting, Optimal, and Overfitting",     x = "Iteration", y = "MSE", color = NULL   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: aml dataset contains 824 differentially methylated regions (DMRs) × 135 samples Acute Myeloid Leukemia study. Features genomic coordinate ranges (e.g., chr1:847816-848609) representing CpG-dense regions methylation beta values. Sample subtypes stored attr(aml, "metadata_h")$category. Since AML features genomic coordinate ranges (e.g., chr1:847816-848609) rather gene names, table top loadings per factor directly interpretable without external annotation databases. Instead, visualize H matrix (sample embeddings) — factor structure maps directly AML subtypes — using pheatmap, handles clustering annotation alignment automatically:  H heatmap reveals clear subtype structure: pheatmap hierarchically clusters rows (factors) columns (samples), aligns subtype annotation bar clustered column order automatically. Certain factors activate preferentially within specific AML subtypes, indicating NMF captures biologically meaningful methylation programs.  stacked bar chart provides complementary view heatmap: subtype’s bar shows relative contribution factor, making subtype-specific factor dominance immediately visible.
code: data(aml) meta <- attr(aml, "metadata_h") model_aml <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5) library(pheatmap)  H <- model_aml$h subtypes <- meta$category  # pheatmap needs unique column identifiers for annotation mapping colnames(H) <- paste0("S", seq_len(ncol(H))) anno_col <- data.frame(Subtype = subtypes, row.names = colnames(H))  # Define subtype colors subtype_levels <- sort(unique(subtypes)) subtype_colors <- setNames(   RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, length(subtype_levels)), "Set2")[seq_along(subtype_levels)],   subtype_levels )  pheatmap(H,          annotation_col = anno_col,          annotation_colors = list(Subtype = subtype_colors),          cluster_cols = TRUE,          cluster_rows = TRUE,          show_colnames = FALSE,          color = viridis::viridis(100),          main = "NMF Sample Embeddings (H) by AML Subtype",          fontsize = 10) # Stacked bar chart: mean factor activation by subtype H <- model_aml$h subtypes <- meta$category  mean_H <- sapply(unique(subtypes), function(s) {   rowMeans(H[, subtypes == s, drop = FALSE]) }) colnames(mean_H) <- unique(subtypes)  bar_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(colnames(mean_H), function(s) {   data.frame(Subtype = s, Factor = paste0("F", 1:nrow(mean_H)),              Activation = mean_H[, s]) }))  ggplot(bar_df, aes(x = Subtype, y = Activation, fill = Factor)) +   geom_col(position = "fill", width = 0.7) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   labs(title = "Factor Composition by AML Subtype",        subtitle = "Proportional factor activation reveals subtype-specific methylation programs",        y = "Proportion of Total Activation", x = NULL) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: compare random vs. Lanczos initialization tracking loss per iteration using on_iteration callback: Convergence comparison: random vs. Lanczos initialization AML data.  Lanczos initialization starts better initial point (SVD-based warm start) typically reaches low loss fewer iterations, random initialization requires iterations reach comparable solution. note local minima: NMF non-convex problem, every initialization converges local minimum. SVD-based initializations deterministically find single good starting point, point may sit basin globally optimal. Running many random initializations samples wider landscape can discover better local minima SVD path misses entirely. practice, common strategy run several random restarts keep solution lowest loss.
code: # Track loss over iterations via callback track_loss <- function() {   losses <- numeric(0)   callback <- function(iter, train_loss, test_loss) {     losses[iter] <<- train_loss   }   list(callback = callback, get = function() losses) }  tracker_random <- track_loss() model_random <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, tol = 1e-6,                     maxit = 80, on_iteration = tracker_random$callback, resource = "cpu")  tracker_lanczos <- track_loss() set.seed(42) model_lanczos <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = "lanczos", tol = 1e-6,                      maxit = 80, on_iteration = tracker_lanczos$callback, resource = "cpu")  loss_random <- tracker_random$get() loss_lanczos <- tracker_lanczos$get()  convergence_df <- rbind(   data.frame(Iteration = seq_along(loss_random), Loss = loss_random, Init = "Random"),   data.frame(Iteration = seq_along(loss_lanczos), Loss = loss_lanczos, Init = "Lanczos") ) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Initialization = c("Random", "Lanczos"),     Iterations = c(length(loss_random), length(loss_lanczos)),     `Final Loss` = round(c(tail(loss_random, 1), tail(loss_lanczos, 1)), 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Convergence comparison: random vs. Lanczos initialization on AML data." ) ggplot(convergence_df, aes(x = Iteration, y = Loss, color = Init)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_y_log10() +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     title = "Convergence: Random vs. Lanczos Initialization",     x = "Iteration", y = "Reconstruction Loss (log scale)", color = "Initialization"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Deep-dive cross-validation: Example 2 introduced rank selection synthetic data. See Cross-Validation vignette real-data workflows, repeated CV, sparse vs. dense masking strategies. Non-Gaussian data: Count data, ratings, overdispersed data need distribution-aware NMF. See Distributions vignette. Factor structure: Control sparsity, smoothness, factor properties. See Regularization vignette.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: Unregularized NMF often produces dense factors every feature loads every component. result hard interpret — factors overlap extensively capture redundant structure. Regularization gives control: sparse factors (active features per component), decorrelated factors (minimal redundancy components), smooth factors (respecting spatial network structure data). RcppML provides six distinct penalty mechanisms, applied independently W H.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: L1 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — LASSO penalty, range [0, 1]. Drives small coefficients exactly zero, producing element-wise sparsity. L2 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Ridge penalty, range ≥ 0. Shrinks large coefficients toward zero without producing exact zeros. L21 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Group sparsity, range ≥ 0. Zeros entire rows columns W H, eliminating full features samples factor.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: angular = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Orthogonality penalty, range ≥ 0. Penalizes correlation factor pairs, encouraging decorrelated components. graph_W, graph_H — Sparse graph Laplacian matrices (dgCMatrix). Penalizes differences connected nodes, enforcing spatial network smoothness. graph_lambda = c(w_strength, h_strength) — Controls strength graph regularization.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: upper_bound = c(w_bound, h_bound) — Maximum coefficient value. Useful bounded data probabilities normalized scores. nonneg = c(TRUE, TRUE) — Nonnegativity toggle. Set FALSE allow negative coefficients (semi-NMF).

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: penalty parameters accept either single value (applied W H) length-2 vector c(w, h) independent control. Zero means penalty (default ). penalty modifies NNLS subproblem: L1 subtracts RHS (soft thresholding), L2 adds Gram diagonal (Tikhonov), angular applies post-solve projection subtracts correlated components factors (decorrelation), graph Laplacian adds Laplacian matrix Gram (smoothness).

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: NMF solves ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H alternating NNLS updates. penalty extends per-column subproblem: minh≥0∥Gh−b∥2+λ1∥h∥1+λ2∥h∥22\min_{h \geq 0} \|Gh - b\|^2 + \lambda_1 \|h\|_1 + \lambda_2 \|h\|^2_2 full penalized NMF objective : ℒ=∥−WDH∥F2+λL1∑ij|Wij|+λL2∑ijWij2+λang∑<jcos(wi,wj)+λGtr(HLH⊤)\mathcal{L} = \|- WDH\|_F^2 + \lambda_{L1} \sum_{ij} |W_{ij}| + \lambda_{L2} \sum_{ij} W_{ij}^2 + \lambda_{\text{ang}} \sum_{< j} \cos(w_i, w_j) + \lambda_G \, \text{tr}(H L H^\top) penalty modifies NNLS subproblem: L1 (LASSO): λ1∥h∥1\lambda_1 \|h\|_1 — soft-thresholds RHS, driving small coefficients exactly zero. Ideal sparse factors. L2 (Ridge): λ2∥h∥22\lambda_2 \|h\|_2^2 — adds λ2I\lambda_2 Gram diagonal (Tikhonov regularization). Shrinks coefficients smoothly. L21 (Group LASSO): λ21∑g∥hg∥2\lambda_{21} \sum_g \|h_g\|_2 — zeros entire groups (rows/columns). Useful feature selection. Angular: λa∑<jwi⊤wj∥wi∥∥wj∥\lambda_a \sum_{<j} \frac{w_i^\top w_j}{\|w_i\|\|w_j\|} — NNLS solve, subtracts correlated components factor profile, directly pushing overlapping factors apart. Graph Laplacian: λGtr(HLH⊤)\lambda_G \, \text{tr}(H L H^\top) L=D−AL = D - — adds Laplacian Gram, encouraging connected nodes share similar values.

what: Example 1: L1 Sparsity Spectrum on Swimmers
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: swimmer benchmark generates 32×32 images stick figures 4 limbs 4 positions (true rank = 16). example images dataset:  fit NMF four L1 levels see sparsity affects factor interpretability. models share random initialization (seed = 42).  L1 = 0 (penalty), factors dense hard interpret. L1 increases, coefficients driven exactly zero — factor isolates specific limb position. Beyond L1 = 0.5, factors become -sparse lose structure.  progression clear: L1 = 0 produces diffuse factors; L1 = 0.1 yields clean, localized limb positions; L1 = 0.5 produces sparsest factors.
code: sim <- simulateSwimmer(256, style = "gaussian", seed = 42, return_factors = TRUE) A <- t(sim$A)  # pixels (1024) x images (256)  # Show 8 example swimmer images example_ids <- round(seq(1, 256, length.out = 8)) example_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(example_ids), function(i) {   idx <- example_ids[i]   grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:32, y = 1:32)   grid$value <- A[, idx]   grid$image <- paste0("Image ", idx)   grid })) example_data$image <- factor(example_data$image, levels = unique(example_data$image))  ggplot(example_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_wrap(~ image, nrow = 1) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) +   ggtitle("Example swimmer images") l1_values <- c(0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5) results <- lapply(l1_values, function(l1) {   nmf(A, k = 16, L1 = c(l1, 0), seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200, verbose = FALSE) })  sparsity_df <- data.frame(   L1 = l1_values,   Sparsity = sapply(results, function(m) 100 * mean(m@w == 0)) ) ggplot(sparsity_df, aes(x = L1, y = Sparsity)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 3) +   labs(x = "L1 penalty", y = "W sparsity (% exact zeros)",        title = "L1 penalty drives factor sparsity") +   theme_minimal() # Use L1 = 0.1 as reference (all 16 factors alive; L1 = 0 can have dead factors) ref_model <- results[[which(l1_values == 0.1)]]  # Select 4 diverse factors (lowest mean off-diagonal cosine similarity) ref_cos <- cosine(ref_model@w) diag(ref_cos) <- 0 ref_mean_sim <- colMeans(ref_cos) factor_ids <- order(ref_mean_sim)[1:4]  # For each L1 model, find best greedy cosine match to each reference factor plot_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(l1_values), function(i) {   model <- results[[i]]   used <- integer(0)   do.call(rbind, lapply(factor_ids, function(fi) {     if (l1_values[i] == 0.1) {       best <- fi  # reference model: show factor directly     } else {       cos_sims <- as.vector(cosine(ref_model@w[, fi, drop = FALSE], model@w))       cos_sims[used] <- -Inf  # exclude already-matched       best <- which.max(cos_sims)     }     used <<- c(used, best)     vals <- rescale01(model@w[, best])     grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:32, y = 1:32)     grid$value <- vals     grid$factor <- paste("Factor", fi)     grid$L1 <- paste0("L1 = ", l1_values[i])     grid   })) })) plot_data$L1 <- factor(plot_data$L1, levels = paste0("L1 = ", l1_values))  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_grid(factor ~ L1) +   coord_equal() +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) +   ggtitle("Swimmer factors at increasing L1 sparsity")

what: Example 2: Angular Decorrelation on Face Data
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: factors high cosine similarity, capture overlapping structure. Angular regularization penalizes pairwise cosine similarity factors, producing distinct components. Angular acts W (factor profiles), decorrelating directions factors occupy feature space without penalizing loading matrix H.  Angular = 0.1 reduces mean -diagonal cosine similarity half 20 factors remain alive reconstruction cost increases modestly. penalty operates via post-solve projection step subtracts correlated components factor NNLS update, directly decorrelating factor profiles.  baseline heatmap shows substantial -diagonal similarity — many factor pairs share structure. Angular regularization reduces -diagonal entries, producing distinct factors without killing .  five best-matched factor pairs show angular regularization reshapes factor. penalty strength, factors shift noticeably — penalty doesn’t just tweak magnitudes changes spatial features factor emphasizes, producing distinct components.
code: data(olivetti) A_faces <- t(olivetti)  # 4096 pixels x 400 images (40 subjects × 10 poses)  baseline <- nmf(A_faces, k = 20, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200) decorr <- nmf(A_faces, k = 20, angular = c(0.1, 0), seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200)  # Align decorrelated factors to baseline via bipartite matching on cosine distance decorr <- align(decorr, baseline)  # Compute factor cosine similarity matrices cos_base <- cosine(baseline@w) cos_decorr <- cosine(decorr@w)  # Mean off-diagonal cosine similarity mean_cos_base <- mean(cos_base[upper.tri(cos_base)]) mean_cos_decorr <- mean(cos_decorr[upper.tri(cos_decorr)])  cos_compare <- data.frame(   Model = factor(rep(c("Baseline", "Angular (0.1)"), each = 2),                  levels = c("Baseline", "Angular (0.1)")),   Metric = rep(c("Mean cosine similarity", "Reconstruction MSE"), 2),   Value = c(mean_cos_base, evaluate(baseline, A_faces),             mean_cos_decorr, evaluate(decorr, A_faces)) ) ggplot(cos_compare, aes(x = Model, y = Value, fill = Model)) +   geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +   facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(y = NULL, title = "Angular regularization: cosine similarity and reconstruction cost") # Build cosine similarity heatmap data for both models (all 20 factors) make_cos_df <- function(cos_mat, label) {   n <- nrow(cos_mat)   grid <- expand.grid(Factor1 = 1:n, Factor2 = 1:n)   grid$similarity <- as.vector(cos_mat)   grid$method <- label   grid }  heatmap_data <- rbind(   make_cos_df(cos_base, "Baseline"),   make_cos_df(cos_decorr, "Angular (0.1)") )  ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = Factor1, y = Factor2, fill = similarity)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B", limits = c(0, 1), oob = scales::squish) +   facet_wrap(~ method) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(aspect.ratio = 1) +   labs(fill = "Cosine\nsimilarity", title = "Factor cosine similarity: Baseline vs. Angular regularization") # Helper to build a face image data.frame with correct orientation face_img_df <- function(vals, factor_label, method_label) {   img <- t(matrix(vals, 64, 64))   grid <- expand.grid(Row = 1:64, Col = 1:64)   grid$Value <- as.vector(img)   grid$Factor <- factor_label   grid$Method <- method_label   grid }  # Select 5 best-matched factor pairs (highest cosine similarity after alignment) cross_cos <- cosine(baseline@w, decorr@w) pair_sim <- diag(cross_cos) best5 <- order(pair_sim, decreasing = TRUE)[1:5]  face_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(best5, function(fi) {   rbind(     face_img_df(rescale01(baseline@w[, fi]), paste("Factor", fi), "Baseline"),     face_img_df(rescale01(decorr@w[, fi]), paste("Factor", fi), "Angular (0.1)")   ) })) face_data$Method <- factor(face_data$Method, levels = c("Baseline", "Angular (0.1)"))  ggplot(face_data, aes(x = Col, y = rev(Row), fill = Value)) +   geom_raster() +   facet_grid(Method ~ Factor) +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "black", high = "white") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 9)) +   ggtitle("Best-matched NMF face factors: Baseline vs. Angular") +   coord_equal()

previous_headings: Example 2: Angular Decorrelation on Face Data
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: choose angular penalty strength, sweep parameter monitor cosine similarity–reconstruction trade-:  sweep shows smooth trade-: mean max cosine similarity decrease monotonically 20 factors remain alive across entire range. Reconstruction cost rises gently — angular = 0.1, MSE increases modestly mean cosine similarity drops half. mild angular (around 0.02) can even improve reconstruction breaking factor redundancy. hallmark well-behaved regularizer: predictable, stable, easy tune.
code: angular_vals <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.05) sweep_results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(angular_vals, function(a) {   m <- nmf(A_faces, k = 20, angular = c(a, 0), seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200)   cos_m <- cosine(m@w)   data.frame(     Angular = a,     CosineSim = mean(cos_m[upper.tri(cos_m)]),     MSE = evaluate(m, A_faces),     MaxCos = max(cos_m[upper.tri(cos_m)])   ) }))  sweep_long <- rbind(   data.frame(Angular = sweep_results$Angular,              Metric = "Reconstruction MSE", Value = sweep_results$MSE),   data.frame(Angular = sweep_results$Angular,              Metric = "Mean cosine similarity", Value = sweep_results$CosineSim),   data.frame(Angular = sweep_results$Angular,              Metric = "Max cosine similarity", Value = sweep_results$MaxCos) ) ggplot(sweep_long, aes(x = Angular, y = Value)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 2) +   facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +   labs(x = "Angular penalty strength", y = NULL,        title = "Angular penalty sweep (W only, 0\u20130.5)") +   theme_minimal()

previous_headings: Example 2: Angular Decorrelation on Face Data
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: Angular regularization reduces factor similarity keeps dense — factor still activates pixels. L1 sparsity produces qualitatively different result: parts instead wholes. moderate L1, factor localizes specific facial feature (eyes, nose, mouth, jaw) rather capturing full-face template.  L1 increases, reconstruction error rises W becomes dramatically sparser. L1 = 0.3, factor activates compact facial region rather entire face — signature parts-based decomposition.  row tracks baseline factor across L1 strengths finding maximum cosine-matching factor L1 model. L1 = 0, factors dense “metafaces” resemble blurry composite face. L1 increases, factor localizes specific facial region — eyes, nose, mouth, chin. L1 = 0.3, result classic NMF parts-based decomposition.
code: # Sweep L1 strengths l1_face_vals <- c(0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3) l1_models <- lapply(l1_face_vals, function(l1) {   nmf(A_faces, k = 20, L1 = c(l1, 0), seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200) })  # Align all models to baseline via bipartite matching l1_models <- lapply(l1_models, function(m) align(m, baseline))  l1_sweep <- data.frame(   L1 = l1_face_vals,   MSE = sapply(l1_models, function(m) evaluate(m, A_faces)),   Sparsity = sapply(l1_models, function(m) 100 * mean(m@w == 0)) )  l1_sweep_long <- rbind(   data.frame(L1 = l1_sweep$L1, Metric = "Reconstruction MSE", Value = l1_sweep$MSE),   data.frame(L1 = l1_sweep$L1, Metric = "W sparsity (% zeros)", Value = l1_sweep$Sparsity) ) ggplot(l1_sweep_long, aes(x = L1, y = Value)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +   facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +   labs(x = "L1 penalty strength", y = NULL,        title = "L1 sweep on faces: reconstruction vs. sparsity tradeoff") +   theme_minimal() # Select 5 diverse baseline factors (lowest mean off-diagonal cosine similarity) baseline_cos <- cosine(baseline@w) diag(baseline_cos) <- 0 mean_sim <- colMeans(baseline_cos) show_factors <- order(mean_sim)[1:5]  # For each L1 model and each baseline factor, find the MAX cosine match parts_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(l1_face_vals), function(li) {   model <- l1_models[[li]]   do.call(rbind, lapply(show_factors, function(fi) {     if (li == 1) {       best <- fi  # baseline: show the factor itself     } else {       cos_sims <- cosine(baseline@w[, fi, drop = FALSE], model@w)       best <- which.max(cos_sims)     }     face_img_df(rescale01(model@w[, best]),                 paste("Factor", fi),                 paste0("L1 = ", l1_face_vals[li]))   })) })) parts_data$Method <- factor(parts_data$Method,                             levels = paste0("L1 = ", l1_face_vals))  ggplot(parts_data, aes(x = Col, y = rev(Row), fill = Value)) +   geom_raster() +   facet_grid(Factor ~ Method) +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "black", high = "white") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 9)) +   ggtitle("Face factors across L1 penalties") +   coord_equal()

what: Example 3: Graph Laplacian for Spatial Smoothness
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: data spatial network structure, graph Laplacian regularization encourages connected nodes share similar factor values. especially useful spatial transcriptomics, tissue spots known physical coordinates standard NMF agnostic spatial arrangement. demonstrate 10X Visium mouse brain dataset SeuratData. requires SeuratData Seurat packages. installed, code blocks silently skipped — install SeuratData stxBrain.SeuratData see spatial results.  λ increases, graph loss decreases (smoother factor loadings) reconstruction error rises — classic regularization tradeoff. spatial effect graph regularization visible trajectory across λ values. baseline factor, find maximum cosine-matching factor regularized model track spatial patterns evolve increasing regularization.  λ = 0, factors show spatial patterns arise purely gene co-expression. λ increases, graph Laplacian penalty encourages neighboring spots similar loadings, producing smoother spatial domains fewer isolated outlier spots.  graph Laplacian loss tr(𝐡L𝐡⊤)\text{tr}(\mathbf{h} L \mathbf{h}^\top) directly measures much factor’s spatial loadings vary neighboring spots. Graph regularization systematically reduces metric across factors, confirming penalty produces factors smoother spatial patterns. metric optimizer targets — reduction guaranteed construction.
code: brain <- SeuratData::LoadData("stxBrain", type = "anterior1") brain <- Seurat::NormalizeData(brain, normalization.method = "LogNormalize",                                scale.factor = 1e4, verbose = FALSE) coords <- Seurat::GetTissueCoordinates(brain) A_norm <- SeuratObject::GetAssayData(brain, layer = "data") A_brain <- as(A_norm, "dgCMatrix")  # Build 6-nearest-neighbor spatial graph from tissue coordinates xy <- as.matrix(coords[, c("x", "y")]) n_spots <- nrow(xy) k_nn <- 6 dist_mat <- as.matrix(dist(xy)) adj <- Matrix::sparseMatrix(i = integer(0), j = integer(0), x = numeric(0),                             dims = c(n_spots, n_spots)) for (i in seq_len(n_spots)) {   neighbors <- order(dist_mat[i, ])[2:(k_nn + 1)]   adj[i, neighbors] <- 1   adj[neighbors, i] <- 1 } degree <- Matrix::rowSums(adj) laplacian <- Matrix::Diagonal(x = degree) - adj laplacian <- as(laplacian, "dgCMatrix")  # Graph regularization loss: tr(D*H L H'*D) / k, accounting for d-scaling graph_reg_loss <- function(model, L) {   mean(sapply(seq_len(nrow(model@h)), function(f) {     h <- model@d[f] * model@h[f, ]     as.numeric(t(h) %*% L %*% h)   })) }  # Sweep lambda: reconstruction vs. smoothness tradeoff k_brain <- 15 lambda_vals <- c(0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5) graph_models <- lapply(lambda_vals, function(lam) {   nmf(A_brain, k = k_brain, graph_H = laplacian,       graph_lambda = c(0, lam), seed = 42, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200) }) graph_sweep <- data.frame(   lambda = lambda_vals,   MSE = sapply(graph_models, function(m) evaluate(m, A_brain)),   graph_loss = sapply(graph_models, function(m) graph_reg_loss(m, laplacian)) )  # Extract baseline and lambda=0.5 models for comparison baseline_g <- graph_models[[1]] smooth_g <- graph_models[[which(lambda_vals == 0.5)]]  # Two-panel sweep plot graph_sweep_long <- rbind(   data.frame(lambda = graph_sweep$lambda, Metric = "Reconstruction MSE",              Value = graph_sweep$MSE),   data.frame(lambda = graph_sweep$lambda, Metric = "Graph loss (tr(HLH')/k)",              Value = graph_sweep$graph_loss) ) graph_sweep_long$lambda_f <- factor(graph_sweep_long$lambda) ggplot(graph_sweep_long, aes(x = lambda_f, y = Value, group = 1)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +   facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +   labs(x = expression("Graph regularization strength ("*lambda*")"), y = NULL,        title = "Graph Laplacian sweep: reconstruction vs. spatial smoothness") +   theme_minimal() # Trajectory: factors as rows, lambda values as columns traj_lambdas <- c(0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.5) traj_models <- lapply(traj_lambdas, function(lam) {   idx <- which(lambda_vals == lam)   graph_models[[idx]] })  # Select 4 diverse baseline factors (lowest mean off-diagonal cosine similarity) base_cos <- cosine(baseline_g@w) diag(base_cos) <- 0 base_mean_sim <- colMeans(base_cos) show_factors <- order(base_mean_sim)[1:4]  # Build spatial trajectory data using max-cosine matching traj_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(traj_lambdas), function(li) {   model <- traj_models[[li]]   do.call(rbind, lapply(show_factors, function(fi) {     if (li == 1) {       best <- fi  # baseline: show the factor itself     } else {       cos_sims <- cosine(baseline_g@w[, fi, drop = FALSE], model@w)       best <- which.max(cos_sims)     }     h_vals <- rescale01(model@h[best, ])     data.frame(       x = xy[, 1], y = xy[, 2], loading = h_vals,       Factor = paste("Factor", fi),       Lambda = paste0("\u03bb = ", traj_lambdas[li])     )   })) })) traj_data$Lambda <- factor(traj_data$Lambda,                            levels = paste0("\u03bb = ", traj_lambdas))  ggplot(traj_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = loading)) +   geom_point(size = 1.0, shape = 16, stroke = 0) +   scale_color_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +   facet_grid(Factor ~ Lambda) +   coord_fixed() +   labs(color = "Loading", x = NULL, y = NULL,        title = "Spatial factor trajectory across graph regularization strength") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8),         axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank()) # Graph Laplacian loss per factor: tr(d*h L h'*d) for each factor graph_loss_per_factor <- function(model, L) {   sapply(seq_len(nrow(model@h)), function(f) {     h <- model@d[f] * model@h[f, ]     as.numeric(t(h) %*% L %*% h)   }) }  gl_base <- graph_loss_per_factor(baseline_g, laplacian) gl_smooth <- graph_loss_per_factor(smooth_g, laplacian)  gl_df <- data.frame(   Model = factor(c(rep("Baseline", k_brain),                    rep("Graph (\u03bb = 0.5)", k_brain)),                  levels = c("Baseline", "Graph (\u03bb = 0.5)")),   GraphLoss = c(gl_base, gl_smooth),   Factor = rep(seq_len(k_brain), 2) )  ggplot(gl_df, aes(x = Model, y = GraphLoss)) +   geom_violin(width = 0.7, fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.7) +   geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +   labs(x = NULL, y = expression("Graph Laplacian loss: tr(h L h'"*")"),        title = "Graph regularization reduces spatial roughness") +   theme_minimal()

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: See NMF Fundamentals vignette core NMF API. See Factor Graphs vignette regularization within composable graph architectures.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: R’s built-serialization formats (.rds, .rda) general-purpose inefficient sparse matrices. serialize entire CSC structure including slot metadata, inflates file sizes slows /O large datasets. StreamPress (.spz) columnar compressed format designed specifically sparse matrices. uses rANS entropy coding delta-encoded indices achieve 5–10× compression raw CSC binary typical scRNA-seq count data, larger gains .rds files. Beyond storage savings, SPZ also faster read raw float32 CSC binary — even single-threaded — dominant cost loading large sparse matrix constructing R/Eigen sparse object raw index value arrays, transferring bytes disk. SPZ parallelises reconstruction step across chunks, raw CSC must assembled sequentially. dense matrices, StreamPress v3 provides optional FP16, QUANT8, rANS compression codecs.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_write() compresses sparse dgCMatrix .spz format: default chunk_bytes = 8e6 sets chunk size ~50 columns typical 38 k-row scRNA-seq matrix, yielding ~60 chunks per sample file. sweet spot parallel decompression: enough chunks real multi-core speedup, chunk large enough per-chunk overhead negligible. Use chunk_bytes = 64e6 (larger) writing small matrices chunk overhead dominate. Returns invisible list compression statistics: raw_bytes, compressed_bytes, ratio, compress_ms.
code: stats <- st_write(x, path,   precision = "auto",         # "fp64", "fp32", "fp16", "quant8", or "auto"   include_transpose = TRUE,   # store CSC(A^T) for fast row access   delta = TRUE,               # delta prediction for row indices   value_pred = FALSE,         # value prediction for integer data   chunk_cols = NULL,          # columns per chunk (NULL = auto from chunk_bytes)   chunk_bytes = 8e6,          # target bytes per chunk (8 MB default)   verbose = FALSE             # print compression statistics )

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_read() decompresses .spz file dgCMatrix: threads = NULL (default) automatically selects thread count based file size. individual sample files (typically < 10 MB compressed), multi-threading adds overhead without measurable benefit — decompression throughput ~21 MB/s per thread thread fork/join costs dominate. large concatenated files (≥ 50 MB), available cores used. cols parameter enables partial reads — load columns need without decompressing entire file.
code: mat <- st_read(path,   cols = NULL,       # column indices to read (NULL = all)   reorder = TRUE,    # undo any row permutation   threads = NULL     # NULL = auto (1 thread for <50 MB, all cores for ≥50 MB) )

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_info() reads header, decompression:
code: info <- st_info(path) # Returns: rows, cols, nnz, density_pct, file_bytes, raw_bytes, ratio, #          version, value_type, chunk_cols, num_chunks, ...

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress v3 handles dense matrices optional compression codecs:
code: st_write_dense(x, path,   codec = "raw",              # "raw", "fp16", "quant8", "fp16_rans", "fp32_rans"   include_transpose = FALSE,  # store transposed panels   chunk_cols = 2048L          # columns per chunk ) mat <- st_read_dense(path)

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress stores column independently CSC-like layout. design enables: Random column access: Read column jj without decompressing columns 1 j−1j-1. Streaming /O: Process columns chunks --core NMF datasets larger RAM. Parallel decompression: Independent columns can decoded concurrently.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: column, StreamPress applies: Delta encoding row indices: Store differences consecutive indices rather absolute values. dense rows, deltas mostly 1 — tiny integers compress extremely well. rANS entropy coding: asymmetric numeral system approaches Shannon entropy bound, yielding near-optimal compression. Precision reduction (optional): Convert 64-bit doubles 32-bit floats, 16-bit half-precision, 8-bit quantized values entropy coding.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: integer count data (UMI counts, ratings), fp32 fp16 lossless limits shown. quant8 maps values 256 equally-spaced buckets, introducing quantization error proportional data range.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: write MovieLens ratings matrix StreamPress verify lossless recovery. MovieLens storage: R object vs. .rds vs. .spz  .spz format preserves numeric values CSC structure exactly. Dimension names (row/column labels) stored .spz file ; use R attributes sidecar metadata .
code: data(movielens)  # Write to StreamPress spz_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") stats <- st_write(movielens, spz_file, include_transpose = FALSE)  # Inspect the file info <- st_info(spz_file)  # Read back ml_back <- st_read(spz_file)  # Compare file sizes rds_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") saveRDS(movielens, rds_file)  size_df <- data.frame(   Format = c("R object (in memory)", ".rds file", ".spz file"),   `Size (KB)` = c(     round(as.numeric(object.size(movielens)) / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(rds_file)$size / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(spz_file)$size / 1024, 1)   ),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(size_df, align = "lr",              caption = "MovieLens storage: R object vs. .rds vs. .spz") size_df$Format <- factor(size_df$Format, levels = rev(size_df$Format)) ggplot(size_df, aes(x = Format, y = `Size (KB)`, fill = Format)) +   geom_col(width = 0.6) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "grey60", "grey80"), guide = "none") +   coord_flip() +   labs(title = "MovieLens Storage Comparison", x = "", y = "Size (KB)") +   theme_minimal() # Verify values are identical (dimnames may differ since SPZ doesn't store them) cat("Values identical:", identical(movielens@x, ml_back@x), "\n") #> Values identical: TRUE cat("Structure identical:", identical(movielens@i, ml_back@i) &&       identical(movielens@p, ml_back@p), "\n") #> Structure identical: TRUE

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress v3 handles dense matrices optional compression codecs. AML dense matrix storage comparison
code: data(aml)  # Write dense matrix dense_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") st_write_dense(aml, dense_file)  # Read back aml_back <- st_read_dense(dense_file)  # Compare sizes rds_dense <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") saveRDS(aml, rds_dense)  dense_df <- data.frame(   Format = c("R object (in memory)", ".rds file", ".spz v3 file"),   `Size (KB)` = c(     round(as.numeric(object.size(aml)) / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(rds_dense)$size / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(dense_file)$size / 1024, 1)   ),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(dense_df, align = "lr",              caption = "AML dense matrix storage comparison") cat("Max absolute difference:", max(abs(aml - aml_back)), "\n") #> Max absolute difference: 2.98015e-08

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: Different precision modes trade file size numerical accuracy. write MovieLens data precision level measure error introduced. Precision vs. compression tradeoff (MovieLens ratings) integer rating data (values 1–5), fp64, fp32, fp16 lossless values fit within exact representation range format. quant8 introduces quantization error, mapping continuous value range 256 discrete buckets.
code: precisions <- c("fp64", "fp32", "fp16", "quant8") prec_results <- data.frame(   Precision = precisions,   `File Size (KB)` = NA_real_,   `Max Abs Error` = NA_real_,   Lossless = NA_character_,   check.names = FALSE,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  for (i in seq_along(precisions)) {   f <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz")   st_write(movielens, f, precision = precisions[i], include_transpose = FALSE)   back <- st_read(f)   err <- max(abs(movielens@x - back@x))   prec_results$`File Size (KB)`[i] <- round(file.info(f)$size / 1024, 1)   prec_results$`Max Abs Error`[i] <- round(err, 6)   prec_results$Lossless[i] <- if (err == 0) "Yes" else "No" }  knitr::kable(prec_results, align = "lrrr",              caption = "Precision vs. compression tradeoff (MovieLens ratings)")

what: Example 4: Real-World Dataset — pbmc3k Single-Cell RNA-seq
title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: pbmc3k dataset ships StreamPress-compressed raw bytes — representative subset (8,000 genes × 500 cells) 10x Genomics PBMC 3k dataset, demonstrating SPZ enables shipping sparse datasets within CRAN’s tarball size limits. pbmc3k StreamPress file summary
code: # Load compressed bytes (not a matrix — raw bytes) data(pbmc3k, package = "RcppML")  # Write to temp file and inspect pbmc3k_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, pbmc3k_file) pbmc_info <- st_info(pbmc3k_file)  pbmc_df <- data.frame(   Property = c("Rows (genes)", "Columns (cells)", "Non-zeros",                "Density", "SPZ file size", "Compression ratio"),   Value = c(     format(pbmc_info$rows, big.mark = ","),     format(pbmc_info$cols, big.mark = ","),     format(pbmc_info$nnz, big.mark = ","),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$density_pct, 1), "%"),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$file_bytes / 1024, 0), " KB"),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$ratio, 1), "x")   ),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) knitr::kable(pbmc_df, align = "lr", caption = "pbmc3k StreamPress file summary") # Load into R and run a quick NMF on a subset counts <- st_read(pbmc3k_file)  # Subset for speed: 500 most variable genes × all cells # Compute row variance efficiently: Var = E[x^2] - E[x]^2 n <- ncol(counts) row_means <- Matrix::rowMeans(counts) row_sq_means <- Matrix::rowMeans(counts^2) gene_var <- row_sq_means - row_means^2 top_genes <- order(gene_var, decreasing = TRUE)[1:500] counts_sub <- counts[top_genes, ]  model <- nmf(counts_sub, k = 5, seed = 42, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 50,              verbose = FALSE) cat("NMF complete: k =", ncol(model@w), ", loss =",     round(model@misc$loss, 2), "\n") #> NMF complete: k = 5 , loss = 14335408

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: datasets large fit memory, StreamPress enables streaming NMF processes matrix chunks directly disk: Streaming reads chunks columns .spz file, updates factor matrices incrementally, discards chunk loading next. enables NMF datasets 10–100× larger available RAM.
code: # Stream NMF from a large .spz file # The matrix is never fully loaded into RAM model <- nmf("/path/to/huge_matrix.spz", k = 20, streaming = TRUE,              maxit = 50, seed = 42)

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress always compression win — roughly 5–10× smaller disk raw float32 CSC binary typical scRNA-seq count data (5% density, 38 k genes × 278 k cells benchmark: 5.36× compression, 828 MB vs 4,434 MB). importantly, SPZ also faster read raw CSC binary, even single-threaded.

what: Why SPZ Beats Raw CSC Even at 1 Thread
title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: Reading large sparse matrix three costs: Benchmarked 38,606 × 278,676 matrix (554 M NNZ) using HPC local storage ~4,000 MB/s bandwidth. Raw CSC spends 78% time step 3 — sparseMatrix() must sort indices, validate structure, allocate R objects 554 M non-zeros. SPZ’s parallel chunk decoder equivalent work faster chunks independent require global sort.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: 5,465 independent chunks (51 cols/chunk 38 k rows), SPZ scales efficently ~32 threads: optimal thread count around 32 matrix size; beyond , thread creation cache contention shared node reduce efficiency. 5,465 chunks provide far parallel work reasonable core count, SPZ scaling limited purely OMP overhead, chunk starvation.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: understanding pure IO vs decode trade-(relevant comparing formats cheap reconstruction cost, e.g., pre-built mmap’d binary): cold NFS (500 MB/s), even 4-thread SPZ comfortably beats equivalent raw binary read total wall-clock time. hot-cache local storage ( 4,000 MB/s), raw IO bounded sparseMatrix() construction, SPZ also avoids.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: batch loading many small samples, parallelising across files (one thread per file, many files concurrently) efficient parallelising within file, intra-file parallelism offers limited speedup files chunks.
code: # Default: auto-selects 1 thread for files < 50 MB, all cores for larger files mat <- st_read("sample.spz")  # Large file: explicitly use 32 threads for best throughput mat <- st_read("large_atlas.spz", threads = 32L)  # Batch-load 100 small samples: parallelise across files, 1 thread each # This avoids OMP thread contention and saturates the CPU more efficiently library(parallel) mats <- mclapply(spz_paths, st_read, threads = 1L, mc.cores = 32L)

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress achieves 5–10× compression raw float32 CSC binary typical scRNA-seq count data (5.36× measured 38 k × 279 k matrix). SPZ faster read raw CSC binary, even single-threaded. dominant cost loading large sparse matrix disk /O sparse object construction (sorting, validation, R/Eigen object allocation hundreds millions non-zeros). SPZ’s parallel chunk decoder performs equivalent construction parallel without global sort step. Parallel scaling real substantial. chunk_bytes = 8e6 (default), 38 k-row matrix produces ~5,400 chunks across 279 k columns — far available core count. Speedup 5.2× vs raw CSC 32 threads benchmark hardware. Default chunk_bytes = 8e6 (8 MB) produces ~50 columns per chunk typical scRNA-seq matrices. increase beyond 64 MB: chunks concentrates work kills OMP parallelism. threads = NULL (default) auto-selects 1 thread files < 50 MB available threads larger files. batch loading many small files, parallelise across files mclapply(..., mc.cores = N) pass threads = 1L st_read call. Precision modes offer clear tradeoff: fp64 lossless preservation, fp32/fp16 lossless integer data, quant8 maximum compression controlled error. pbmc3k shipping pattern (raw bytes .rda → writeBin → st_read) enables distributing large datasets within CRAN’s tarball size limits. Streaming NMF .spz files enables analysis datasets larger available RAM. streaming path reads chunks columns, performs NMF updates chunk, discards loading next — peak memory usage proportional chunk size, total matrix size. See GPU Acceleration vignette GPU-direct reads .spz, NMF Fundamentals vignette fitting NMF models loaded data.

title: SVD and PCA
text: Sometimes don’t need nonnegative parts — need axes capture variance. PCA SVD workhorses unconstrained dimensionality reduction: orthogonal, optimal mean squared error, applicable real-valued data. Unlike NMF, PCA components can mixed signs, allowing cancellation capturing signed contrasts (e.g., “high group , low group B”). RcppML’s svd() fast (Eigen-based), sparse-aware, regularizable. Use PCA data mixed signs want orthogonal axes. Use NMF want interpretable nonneg parts. Use sparse PCA want interpretable loadings PCA framework.

title: SVD and PCA
text: Returns S4 object class svd slots: @u — m×km \times k score matrix (left singular vectors) @d — length-kk singular values @v — n×kn \times k loading matrix (right singular vectors) @misc — metadata (including $row_means centered)
code: svd(A, k = 10, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200,     center = FALSE, scale = FALSE,     seed = NULL, threads = 0,     L1 = 0, L2 = 0, nonneg = FALSE,     method = "auto", resource = "auto", ...)

title: SVD and PCA
text: Convenience wrapper: pca(, k, ...) equivalent svd(, k, center = TRUE, ...). Row means stored model@misc$row_means.
